### Post by [LocK] on 2009-02-22
after hours of fighting with kernels, lots of recompilation and reboots ... i can say for me: *RTFM you idiot!*

in kernels >2.6.26 there are no frame buffer mode called "efifb" ... but ... there is "imacfb" (with specified version) for more information see [Documentation/fb/imacfb.txt]("http://www.mjmwired.net/kernel/Documentation/fb/imacfb.txt")

but, now i have another problem becouse, after i passed "video=imacfb:macbook" to kernel arg line, i got "blue screen" :|

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-02-22
> **'[LocK] said:**
> after hours of fighting with kernels, lots of recompilation and reboots ... i can say for me: *RTFM you idiot!*

in kernels >2.6.26 there are no frame buffer mode called "efifb" ... but ... there is "imacfb" (with specified version) for more information see [Documentation/fb/imacfb.txt]("http://www.mjmwired.net/kernel/Documentation/fb/imacfb.txt")

but, now i have another problem becouse, after i passed "video=imacfb:macbook" to kernel arg line, i got "blue screen" :|

Actually, you don't need imacfb with grub2 efi. When grub2 sees

video=efifb
or
video=vesafb

, it calculates the frame buffer address and line length automatically for you, while imacfb rely on fixed value:

M_I17:
      width = 1440
      height = 900
      linelength = 1472 * 4
      base = 0x80010000

M_I20:
	width = 1680
	height = 1050
	linelength = 1728 * 4
	base = 0x80010000

M_MINI:
	width = 1024
	height = 768
	linelength = 2048 * 4
	base = 0x80000000

MACBOOK:
	width = 1280
	height = 800
	linelength = 2048 * 4
	base = 0x80000000

These values are not correct for new models anyway.

### Post by cyberdork33 on 2009-02-23
> **pxwpxw said:**
> Yes similar to other amd64 on 32bit efi.

Could you try the debian lenny amd64 netboot installer images on the MBP4,1 (can just put it on the Mac OSX root or a usb stick see previous post). Works for my MBP4,1.

[http://ftp.nl.debian.org/debian/dists/lenny/main/installer-amd64/current/images/netboot/debian-installer/amd64/](http://ftp.nl.debian.org/debian/dists/lenny/main/installer-amd64/current/images/netboot/debian-installer/amd64/)

[ linux initrd.gz ]
yes, sorry, I hadn't gotten around to that yet. I think that Bean also has a MBP4,1? I thought i saw that somewhere.

> **pepijndevos said:**
> What is the best option to safely get Ubuntu on my Mac?
"Best" is a matter of opinion, and also depending on your goals. The most supported method is to boot with bios emulation and not use grub-efi.
> **pepijndevos said:**
> Is there a way to get it working with my external drive and rEFIt or Grub-efi?
You will have to get it working with grub-efi if you want to boot from an external device. This is a limitation of Apple's firmware bios emulation and "legacy" Operating Systems.
> **pepijndevos said:**
> How safe is it to shrink my mac partition and install it there?(and will it boot without to much fumbling with Kernels, config files and Grub builds)It is quite safe, though you should backup before doing anything of the sort. (Since you have a firewire drive, setting up time machine or cloning your system should be easy. For basic installation instrcutions see:
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/MactelSupportTeam/AppleIntelInstallation](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/MactelSupportTeam/AppleIntelInstallation)

> **shadowdude1794 said:**
> Can someone dumb this down for me? Does this mean it works now? 
thanks
Maybe we should have another thread, or a wiki page with basic instructions to make this work. (wait, didn't we have a wiki page already?)

I had grub.cfg editing problems initially, it is very picky, and does not give any indication of the error, resulting in no menu or part menu or error.

Most difficult to spot is an extra space at the end of some lines
particularly

menuuentry "bad entry" {  <-space there 

or of course a missing closing }

and when the error is down at the bottom of the file it is difficult to notice.

I also get an error message sometimes on restart, claiming menu error, and have to repeat restart, usually helps to go via Option key.

You could post the edited menu if in doubt and I can run it.

Also occasionally rEFIt vanishes and gets restored by booting MacOSX, which in turn lets the Refit Blesser reinstate refit.

Hopefully all this will be tidied up one day, but I will be happy if we can just get ubuntu running properly on 32/64bit apple efi via grub.efi boot.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-02-25
> **pxwpxw said:**
> 
Most difficult to spot is an extra space at the end of some lines
particularly

menuentry "bad entry" { <-space there

or of course a missing closing }

Yes, I notice this as well. However, it used to be working, in fact, you can even write it like this:

menuentry "menu"
{
  ...
}

But this is broken now. I believe it's caused by some recent commit in this year. Although it's not very serious, just a little annoying.

BTW, about the keyboard hang issue, I remember something that could be important. The mac doesn't implement one of the keyboard port properly, which affects bios service. If we read key using int 16/ah=0, it would hang at the second key press. The solution is to use int 16/ah=1 to check for the presence of key before reading it. You can boot some of the old dos utility disc to verify it. Although linux kernel doesn't use bios service, but since the fault is in the port, it could also be affected.

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-02-26
Ok, I've solved it. The problem is in ACPI. When booting 64-bit kernel from 32-bit efi, or vice verse, EFI is actually not usable (efi_enabled = 0 in the kernel), so it falls back to pc mode.

When efi_enabled = 1, the acpi table address is retrieved from efi system table, otherwise, it needs to scan bios data area to find it. I copy the root node of acpi to conventional memory and fake the bios data, so that it can be found just as booting from pc. Now the keyboard hang and hda timeout is gone.

BTW, it seems 2.6.28 amd64 kernel have some problem handling the efi. I disable the efi signature for the time being, which cause efi_enabled = 0 (just like booting from 32-bit firmware). It should be booting now.

---

> Ok, I've solved it. The problem is in ACPI. When booting 64-bit kernel from 32-bit efi, or vice verse, EFI is actually not usable (efi_enabled = 0 in the kernel), so it falls back to pc mode.

When efi_enabled = 1, the acpi table address is retrieved from efi system table, otherwise, it needs to scan bios data area to find it. I copy the root node of acpi to conventional memory and fake the bios data, so that it can be found just as booting from pc. Now the keyboard hang and hda timeout is gone.

BTW, it seems 2.6.28 amd64 kernel have some problem handling the efi. I disable the efi signature for the time being, which cause efi_enabled = 0 (just like booting from 32-bit firmware). It should be booting now.

2.6.28-6-generic x86_64

### Post by bean123 on 2009-02-27
I've found the reason why grub.efi would hang on 2.6.27+ 64-bit kernel. It's in function efi_enter_virtual_mode in arch/x86/kernel/efi.c:

```

void __init efi_enter_virtual_mode(void)
{
        efi_memory_desc_t *md;
        efi_status_t status;
        unsigned long size;
        u64 end, systab, addr, npages;
        void *p, *va;

        efi.systab = NULL;
        for (p = memmap.map; p < memmap.map_end; p += memmap.desc_size) {
                md = p;
                if (!(md->attribute & EFI_MEMORY_RUNTIME))
                        continue;

                size = md->num_pages << EFI_PAGE_SHIFT;
                end = md->phys_addr + size;

                if (PFN_UP(end) <= max_low_pfn_mapped)
                        va = __va(md->phys_addr);
                else
                        va = efi_ioremap(md->phys_addr, size);

```

Here, it tries to map the efi runtime area to virtual addresses. In 2.6.26, the kernel maps the whole 4G memory, which includes the efi runtime area, so it just need to call __va to retrieve the virtual address. But since 2.6.27, the kernel only maps existing physical memory, and the efi runtime is outside this range, so it needs to call efi_ioremap. However, in efi_ioremap:

```

void __iomem *__init efi_ioremap(unsigned long phys_addr, unsigned long size)
 {
         static unsigned pages_mapped __initdata;
         unsigned i, pages;
         unsigned long offset;

        pages = PFN_UP(phys_addr + size) - PFN_DOWN(phys_addr);
        offset = phys_addr & ~PAGE_MASK;
        phys_addr &= PAGE_MASK;

        if (pages_mapped + pages > MAX_EFI_IO_PAGES)
                return NULL;

```

The maximum number of efi pages is limited to MAX_EFI_IO_PAGES, which is set to 100. This is clearly not enough for apple 64-bit firmware. 100 * 4096 = 400K, but the runtime size is about 17M, according the the dmesg output:

```

[    0.000000] EFI: mem187: type=6, attr=0x800000000000000f, range=[0x000000003eeff000-0x000000003ef00000) (0MB)
[    0.000000] EFI: mem188: type=0, attr=0x8000000000000000, range=[0x00000000000a0000-0x00000000000c0000) (0MB)
[    0.000000] EFI: mem189: type=0, attr=0x8000000000000000, range=[0x000000003ef00000-0x0000000040000000) (17MB)
[    0.000000] EFI: mem190: type=11, attr=0x8000000000000000, range=[0x00000000f00f8000-0x00000000f00f9000) (0MB)
[    0.000000] EFI: mem191: type=11, attr=0x8000000000000000, range=[0x00000000fed1c000-0x00000000fed20000) (0MB)
[    0.000000] EFI: mem192: type=11, attr=0x8000000000000000, range=[0x00000000fffa0000-0x00000000fffd0000) (0MB)

```

There are two solution, first is to map the whole 4G area, just like in 2.6.26. In order to do so, you can modify arch/x86/kernel/e820.c, replace these function:

```

unsigned long __init e820_end_of_ram_pfn(void)
{
        return e820_end_pfn(MAX_ARCH_PFN, E820_RAM);
}

unsigned long __init e820_end_of_low_ram_pfn(void)
{
        return e820_end_pfn(1UL<<(32 - PAGE_SHIFT), E820_RAM);
}

```

with these ones:
```

unsigned long __init e820_end_of_ram_pfn(void)
{
        unsigned long r = e820_end_pfn(MAX_ARCH_PFN, E820_RAM);
        return (r > 0x100000) ? r : 0x100000;
}

unsigned long __init e820_end_of_low_ram_pfn(void)
{
        return 0x100000;
}

```

Another solution is to change the efi pages limit MAX_EFI_IO_PAGES, which is defined in arch/x86/include/asm/efi.h. However, when I enlarge MAX_EFI_IO_PAGES, some error occurs compiling the kernel. It seems that we need to adjust other value to accommodate the new MAX_EFI_IO_PAGES.

I adjust the patch a little and send it to grub-devel. In this patch, I re-enable the efi signature, as the cause of hang is not from grub's side. To use it in 2.6.27 64-bit kernel and above, you need to modify the kernel source suggest previously, or add a "noefi" option to linux command line, which disable efi altogether, for example:

```

linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.28-amd64 root=/dev/sdb1 video=efifb agp=off noefi
initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.28-amd64

```

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-02-27
> **cyberdork33 said:**
> I didn't mean from you, sorry. I need a 32bit binary of the patched grub.efi

my post #17 the log of building grub.ef is still ok I think, except the preloaded module list I use (may not be optimum).

../configure --with-platform=efi --target=i386

( you probably get target i386 by default)

For creating grub.efi 
 
preloaded modulelist
```

apple appleldr boot cat chain configfile cpio date ext2 echo fat gpt help hexdump hfs hfsplus iso9660 linux ls normal pc reboot reiserfs scsi search sleep xfs

```

That should run grub.efi standalone with the preloaded modules I think, then full module set not needed, but if wanted - 

Using grub-mkimage -
```
 
 ./grub-mkimage -d . -o grub.efi `cat modulelist`
 mkdir grubdir
 nano grub.cfg
 cp *.efi *.mod grub.cfg grubdir

```

### Post by bean123 on 2009-02-28
> **shadowdude1794 said:**
> Can someone please explain all of this to me?

In short, this is how to make it work:

Suppose firmware is 64-bit:

2.6.26, 686 and amd64
OK

2.6.28, 686
Needs acpi=force option

2.6.28, amd64
Needs noefi option

For all system, option video=efifb agp=off is needed to get a working frame buffer. You may also need to blacklist agppart if booting to X-Window.

---

### Post by cyberdork33 on 2009-02-28
OK, I am posting my setup here for everyone's benefit. Think of this as a kind of draft for my documentation. It is a little unorganized for step by step directions ATM, but I will clean it up.

I have an external hard drive on my iMac5,1 that I use for Time Machine. I also had an extra FAT32 partition that I was using to hold music and such to share between Linux and OS X. This is the drive that I am using for this process.

[LIST=1]
[*]It is very important that your external drive is formatted as a GPT disk. This can be done in Disk Utility using the Options button, or in gparted using Device > Create Partition Table ...
[*]Download the Ubuntu Install disc normally and boot. Install to the external drive as you normally would. You can skip the install of GRUB at the end if you want. (In my case, I wanted to preserve the TimeMachine backup volume, so I used gparted to delete my FAT32 partition and then created the proper root and swap partitions with the installer). When finished, you will nto be able to boot it up.
[*]Install rEFIt onto your Mac OSX partition normally (You can also install it to the EFI partition of the external drive if you know what you are doing.)
[*] I got pxwpxw's grub packages from [here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6767881&postcount=209"). There is a 32bit version and a 64bit version. Depending on your Mac model, you may need either one. [create table of hardware and EFI binary base size]. Unpack the archive. Inside there is a file named "grub32.efi" or grub64.efi You will need to replace it with the correct files from Bean, [here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6812681&postcount=293").
[*]You want to put the directory you unpacked into the EFI partition of the external drive in /efi. rEFIt will be able to see it here.
[*]reboot and in the rEFIt screen choose to boot the grub*.efi file that you put on the external.
[/LIST]
Here is the grub config file I am using. It should be named grub.cfg and placed in the directory with your grub*.efi file. You will need to replace the UUID with yours, and make sure the kernel and initrd file names are correct. Note that I placed a file in the root of my Linux directory called 'ExtDrive' so that I could use grub's search command to find the correct root device (I also have Jaunty installed on my internal hard drive).```
set default=0
set timeout=20

set menu_color_normal=cyan/blue
set menu_color_highlight=white/blue

menuentry "OSX" {
    search --set /usr/standalone/i386/boot.efi
    chainloader /usr/standalone/i386/boot.efi
}

menuentry "Ubuntu on External Disk" {
    search --set /ExtDrive
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.28-8-generic root=UUID=e4c96b6a-591d-4b01-9f5b-e816b3e50d69 ro video=efifb agp=off
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-2.6.28-8-generic
}

menuentry "CD" {
   appleloader CD
}

menuentry "MBR" {
   appleloader HD
}

menuentry "Partition List" {
    ls -l
    sleep 5
}

menuentry "REBOOT" {
    reboot
}
```In order to get xorg working, you have to block the agp modules, and set fbdev as the driver to use in your xorg.conf as explained by pxwpxw [here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6267026&postcount=2").

Try a blind login and reboot to confirm
EDIT easiest check is kernel option break=bottom.
May just need agp=off, or you may need to  blacklist your agp modules.
Also some gotchas when running external from different machines - e.g. my network startup needs reconfig for different hardware (not a grub issue).

Compile on ubuntu 810 
i   gcc                             - The GNU C compiler                        
i   gcc-4.3                         - The GNU C compiler                        
i   gcc-4.3-base                    - The GNU Compiler Collection (base package)
i A gcc-4.3-multilib                - The GNU C compiler (multilib files)       
i   gcc-multilib                    - The GNU C compiler (multilib files)       
i A lib64gcc1                       - GCC support library (64bit)               
i   libgcc1                         - GCC support library        

MacOSX libs not suitable AFAIK, would be nice though.

On this Xserve refit starts only 32bit Grub2 versions (both forums and svn).

grub>root=(hd0,3)

result:

Hi,  welcome - first Xserve seen here.

Well it seems to be almost working.

I looked in a grub2 rev 2008, that message only appears in here:
(I am on the wrong computer for 2010 )

./loader/i386/efi/linux.c:225:	    grub_fatal ("cannot allocate pages");
 in allocate_pages (grub_size_t prot_size) 
./loader/i386/efi/linux.c:982:    grub_fatal ("cannot allocate pages");
 in grub_rescue_cmd_initrd (int argc, char *argv[])

You could have a look at the source or try

set debug=linux

Or add some grub_dprintf( ) debugging.

Thats about all I can suggest.

grub>linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.27-11-generic ro root=/dev/sda3 agp=off video=efifb acpi=force noefi
 
command's result:
   [Linux-bzImage, setup=0x3000, size=0x221ed0]
Video mode: 1280x1024-32@60
Video frame buffer: 0x80010000
Video line length: 5120

grub>initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.27-11-generic

result:
cannot allocate pages
Aborted. Press any key  to exit.

I will be very happy if someone point me a way how to start Linux on Xserve.

How to debug this boot process?
Do I have to compile custom kernel? Is it kernels problem? 

Thank you in advance.

Try this one, it should print some numbers before the "cannot allocate pages" error.

### Post by cyberdork33 on 2009-03-02
> **pxwpxw said:**
> This bug sometimes comes at grub startup, affecting the menu display.
It happens on iMac81 and MBP41.  

1-error
shows syntax error, but the grub.cfg is correct

2-continue
After key  pressed the menu display is truncated

3-restart
Sometimes persistent error requires several restarts
Finally the menu display is correct.

4-grub.cfg.txt

---------------
I can confirm this behavior. I usually drop to the grub commandline and use the configfile command to reload it though.

> **pushken said:**
> As you probably know this machine does not support bootcamp bios emulation.
Thus you cannot start any non-mac installation in traditional way.I actually did not know that. :)

> **pushken said:**
> grub>root=(hd0,3)
grub>linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.27-11-generic ro root=/dev/sda3 agp=off video=efifb acpi=force noefi
 
command's result:
   [Linux-bzImage, setup=0x3000, size=0x221ed0]
Video mode: 1280x1024-32@60
Video frame buffer: 0x80010000
Video line length: 5120

grub>initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.27-11-generic

result:
cannot allocate pages
Aborted. Press any key  to exit.
I got this once on my MBP4,1. I am not sure what the cause was, but I rebooted, and it worked fine the next time.

Also, for everyone, I don't think the agp=off option is needed. I have all the agp modules blacklisted, and have been booting without the agp=off option and it boots just fine into xorg.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-03
> **shadowdude1794 said:**
> Break didn't work. Bean, I'm not sure what you mean. I'm still a little bit of a newb :( .

I suspect that this could be a fs related problem, which results in kernel/initrd not read properly. You can copy them to another partition, like fat, and try again. The location of kernel/initrd does not affect booting, as long as you set the root option properly.

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-03-03
When Mac OSX partitions the USB stick using GUID partitioning, it makes 
 partition #1 200M fat32. This can be used for efi and linux kernel.
Mac OSX wont show it because it is flagged boot.

Then vmlinuz and initrd.img can be copied from /boot to there (renamed) and used by grub.efi to bootlinux
Can be set up fom a ubuntu live cd or a linux OS on HD, but not from Mac OSX.

The sdb1 external location conveniently makes it the first found by 
 search --set /vmlinuz

I tested this on an 8GB stick, with xubuntu810 on /dev/sdb3
(flash has some other test stuff on it). 

I am posting from firefox on flash now, iMac81.

```

pxw@flash:~$ dmesg | grep 'command line' 
[    0.000000] Kernel command line: BOOT_IMAGE=/vmlinuz root=/dev/sdb3 video=efifb acpi=force
pxw@flash:~$ 

pxw@flash:~$ sudo parted /dev/sdb p free
Model: FLASH Drive AU_USB20 (scsi)
Disk /dev/sdb: 8288MB
Sector size (logical/physical): 512B/512B
Partition Table: gpt

Number  Start   End     Size    File system  Name                  Flags
        17.4kB  20.5kB  3072B   Free Space                              
 1      20.5kB  210MB   210MB   fat32        EFI System Partition  boot 
 2      210MB   1056MB  846MB   hfs+         Apple_HFS_Untitled_2       
 3      1056MB  7880MB  6824MB  ext3                                    
 4      7880MB  8283MB  403MB   ext2                                    
        8283MB  8288MB  5071kB  Free Space                              

pxw@flash:~$ 
pxw@flash:~$ sudo mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt
pxw@flash:~$ ls -l /mnt
total 10186
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root    8192 2008-11-23 17:19 efi
-rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 8176539 2008-12-29 01:28 initrd.img
-rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 2244272 2008-10-24 19:29 vmlinuz
pxw@flash:~$ 


```

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-03
> **pxwpxw said:**
> Good one I think, did about 20 quick starts including variety of actions and used so far with no errors. (imac and  mbp). 

(Compared with previous version, 1 bad in 5 starts twice).

EDIT:
Faultless after another 10 or so starts over few hours.

What was it?

Right, this bug is actually caused by gcc. I've post a thread at grub-devel naming "A serious stack issue caused by gcc optimization". You can find the quick fix there.

EDIT: Thanks to phcoder from grub-devel, there is a complete solution for this issue, the patch can be found in the list.

### Post by planetbeing on 2009-03-05
I am trying to get a working installation of Ubuntu on MacBookAir1,2.

I have version 0.12 of rEFIt installed, as well as grub-efi from post #57 and the grub64.efi binary from post #351. If there is a newer package, please let me know (and probably edit OP accordingly). I wasn't able to read all 359 posts in this thread, unfortunately.

I am using a 16 GB USB stick with ubuntu-8.10-desktop-amd64.iso and the files from here: [http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/dists/intrepid/main/installer-amd64/current/images/hd-media/](http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/dists/intrepid/main/installer-amd64/current/images/hd-media/)

GRUB seems to be working fine, and I can execute most commands without problems. I tested the 32-bit version and it didn't work for me, but the 64-bit version seems fine.

I also tried a i386 kernel/initrd set earlier, and GRUB wasn't able to load the initrd, complaining about it not being able to "allocate".

The amd64 kernel/initrd set seems to load: I get a very distorted language selection screen with the following boot options:

root=(hd0,1)
linux /vmlinuz video=efifb noefi
initrd /initrd.gz

There seemed to be three copies of the language selection screen appearing in three "stripes" down the screen, each stripe being a very squished looking copy of the screen. I did not seem to be able to get any response via keyboard.

Adding agp=off, acpi=force, exchanging video=efifb for video=vesafb all seemed to have no effect. noefi was necessary, or the computer seemed to hang before anything happened.

I am willing to spend some time testing or looking into the source code myself, but I'd like a starting point, or some clue as to what might be wrong. What should I do next?

### Post by planetbeing on 2009-03-05
Some partial success for me.

I compiled r2019 from SVN myself. This time, with options: video=vesafb noefi acpi=force, the video is fine and it boots to the language detection menu. Everything works fine until the "Detecting hardware, please wait..." stage where it's "Detecting hardware to find hard drives". At that point, the installation hangs.

However, video=efifb no longer seems to be working. The boot also doesn't seem to happen if acpi=force is not in the command line. There seems to be some sort of race condition, since I end up getting kernel panics with a general protection fault sometimes, or sometimes just complete freeze before I get any video, all with the same options. It's hard to say for sure what's going on.

EDIT: NM, efifb works. It's just that half of the time, I get a kernel panic before the menu comes up. The panic is always in various places in the fs code, like trying to remove an inode or changing the atime. It's very strange. The freeze with the hardware detection also seems to be a race condition.

---

### Post by pushken on 2009-03-06
> **bean123 said:**
> What's your grub.cfg, any syntax problem ?

Bean, you are right.
Mia culpa, I had an error in grub.cfg.

It was "initrd=/initrd" istead of "initrd /initrd" 

Now it boots fine.

Thank you.

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-07
> **pushken said:**
> Bean, you are right.
Mia culpa, I had an error in grub.cfg.

It was "initrd=/initrd" istead of "initrd /initrd" 

Now it boots fine.

Thank you.

Ok, patch committed (r2021).

### Post by PwnWithin on 2009-03-11
hello everyone! I was amazed at the progress I had seen reading this thread from start to finish, so I decided it would be worth an attempt to see if I could get ubuntu running off of a usb flash drive. I'm on a MBP3,1 (4gmem), and where I'm at now is a flash drive partitioned for EFI and the rest contains intrepid x86 sans bootloader. I can boot into grub on the usb drive, but I was wondering how exactly should I configure the menuentry in the .cfg file. flash drive is hd1, EFI hfs partition is hd1,1, ubuntu is installed on hd,1,2. Could the entry really be as simple as:

menuentry "Linux" {
       root=hd1,2
       linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.25 root=/dev/sdb2 video=vesafb agp=off
       initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.25
}

(Taken from the testing on mactel page), or am I really missing something here? Any help as to where to go from here or what I have done incorrectly would be greatly appreciated :D

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-03-11
> **PwnWithin said:**
> hello everyone! I was amazed at the progress I had seen reading this thread from start to finish, so I decided it would be worth an attempt to see if I could get ubuntu running off of a usb flash drive. I'm on a MBP3,1 (4gmem), and where I'm at now is a flash drive partitioned for EFI and the rest contains intrepid x86 sans bootloader. I can boot into grub on the usb drive, but I was wondering how exactly should I configure the menuentry in the .cfg file. flash drive is hd1, EFI hfs partition is hd1,1, ubuntu is installed on hd,1,2. Could the entry really be as simple as:

menuentry "Linux" {
       root=hd1,2
       linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.25 root=/dev/sdb2 video=vesafb agp=off
       initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.25
}

(Taken from the testing on mactel page), or am I really missing something here? Any help as to where to go from here or what I have done incorrectly would be greatly appreciated :D

Hi,

USB stick is usually seen by grubefi as hd0, but easiest check is from 
grub> commandline ('c' from menu)
```

grub> search /vmlinuz

```
Whatever grub says is right.

However, it is still /dev/sdb as seen by linux, so that looks OK.
I assume you are running the grub64.efi on MBP3,1
and have insalled x86 not amd64 (thats fine), so possibly

```

menuentry "Linux" {
root=hd0,2
linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.25 root=/dev/sdb2 video=vesafb agp=off acpi=force single
initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.25
}

```
Not sure whether you need an agp blacklist, it is not needed on mbp41 nor is agp=off.
the 'single' option should get text console so you can edit /etc/X11/xorg.conf to show
 
 Driver "fbdev"

Then carry on to desktop.

you also have to set root differently

```
set root=(hd0,2)

```
search --set /vmlinuz

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-03-13
> **bean123 said:**
> Ok, patch committed (r2021).
Bean,

AGP with grub.efi - what are the prospects?
Can we do anything?

Should I post this on grub-devel?

Apple macs now using grub.efi booting linux must use video frame buffer for the linux console.

32 bit efi macs such as MacBook2,1 circa 2007 require disabling early activation of agp to allow console video  using the framebuffer.

64 bit efi including MBP4,1 IMAC8,1 do not require disabling agp, apparently because is is not activated until after console video is setup with the efifb. This allows easy post install restart to a root console to make any changes.  

To run the gui desktop xserver, all apple efi macs tested so far (there may be sone exceptions) also require graphics to use the xorg fbdev framebuffer device so we set Device "fbdev". The fbdev With no AGP is unsuitable for multimedia video and special effects,  (Fine for me here on a text editor, web browser mail and such). This is stopping grub.efi from being taken seriously by many users who expect preformance equal to OSX and Windows, using proprietary hardware drivers such as fglrx or nvidia.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-16
FINALLY, I figure out a way to make x.org work without frame buffer.

X.org would scan bios rom at 0xc0000 to locate video modes, normally, the area is not writable, but I've found a way to do that, so that you can load a vbios rom image to that address.

First, you need to get bios dump when booting from pc mode. Run the follow command:

```

sudo dd if=/dev/mem of=bios.bin bs=16384 skip=48 count=16

```

Then, booting using grub64.efi from the attachment, there is a new command loadbios which can be used to load bios.bin

```

loadbios (hd0,1)/bios.bin

```

You can use hexdump to verify the bios has been loaded properly:
```

hexdump -s 0xc0000 (mem)

```

If the value are not all 0xff, you're good to go.

Use linux and initrd to load linux like previously, but don't use agp=off or blacklist agppart. The screen would be scramble at console mode, but that's all right. X.org can reinitialize video registers once it's started.

BTW, this also solved the DMI problem, you can check dmesg file to confirm.

---

(EE) NV(0): Couldn't find the DDC routing table.  Mode setting will probably fail!

From the log file, I guess the problem is in vbe:

Previously:
(EE) fglrx(0): V_BIOS address 0x0 out of range
(WW) fglrx(0): GetVBEMode failed
(II) fglrx(0): ATI Video BIOS revision 9 or later detected

Now:
(II) fglrx(0): Primary V_BIOS segment is: 0xc000
(II) Loading sub module "vbe"
(II) LoadModule: "vbe"

Now that I fake the V_BIOS segment, it expects VESA BIOS to be working, which is not the case. While previously, V_BIOS fails, so it falls back to default handling. I notice that radeonhd don't use vbe, perhaps it would go futher.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-19
> **bean123 said:**
> Hi,

I got another idea, it might be possible to enable pci rom directly. Please try this one. Don't use loadbios, just linux /vmlinux video=efifb, it would display the rom address, try hexdump it and see if it's all 0xff.

Hi,

Oh, never mind about that. In fact, even if pci rom can be located, it contains uninitialized code, which is not useful anyway.

I think the trick is to provide a real INT10 address, which is used by to x.org to call vbe function. In fact, you can just get the whole IVT table in pc mode:

```

sudo dd if=/dev/mem of=ivt.bin bs=1024 count=1

```

The loadbios command is extended to load ivt.bin:

```

loadbios /bios.bin /ivt.bin

```

Now I can see VESA BIOS detected in x.org log file.

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-20
> **pxwpxw said:**
> Tried using MBP41 kernel 2.6.27 with the xorg nv driver.

loadbios /bios.bin /ivt.bin

linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda4 

No sign of desktop with nv, but logs show int10.
I could try variations with nv xorg.conf but it works for pc boot.

BTW, the dark black gdm desktop login screen I had with nvidia and 2.6.27 x86 kernel, was not there for nvidia and 2.6.28 amd64.

Keyboard F1 F2 screen brightness works for nvidia pc boot, not availabe for nv. Screen brightness for efi boot with efifb and fbdev is inherited from the OSX setting, not so for pc boot.

Hi,

How about mb81, does the new grub64.efi make any difference ?

BTW, I have located the cause of "Couldn't find the DDC routing table" using x.org source code. NV driver uses information stored in MMIO space to locate rom code, in xf86-video-nv/src/g80_driver.c, G80PreInit:

```

    xf86DrvMsg(pScrn->scrnIndex, X_INFO, "Linear framebuffer mapped at %p\n",
               (void*)pNv->mem);

    pNv->table1 = (unsigned const char*)&pNv->reg[0x00800000/4];
    tmp = pNv->reg[0x00619F04/4] >> 8;
    if(tmp)
        pNv->table1 -= ((pNv->RamAmountKBytes << 10) - (tmp << 16));
    else
        pNv->table1 -= 0x10000;

```

table1 should point to the pci rom area. It's possible to fix it up in grub2, but this is too hardware specific to be useful in general.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-20
BTW, how about blacklist the backlight module ? does it affect pc mode, any change in efi mode ?

EDIT: I take a look at the backlight source code, it's quite simple. I've written a small tool to get/set backlight intensity, please try it out.

```

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/io.h>

static void
mbp_set_intensity (int intensity)
{
  outb (0x04 | (intensity << 4), 0xb3);
  outb (0xbf, 0xb2);
}

static int
mbp_get_intensity (void)
{
  outb (0x03, 0xb3);
  outb (0xbf, 0xb2);
  return inb (0xb3) >> 4;
}

int
main (int argc, char **argv)
{
  if (iopl (3) < 0)
    {
      perror ("No IO permissions");
      return 1;
    }

  printf ("Current intensity: %d\n", mbp_get_intensity ());

  if (argc > 1)
    {
      int n;

      n = atoi (argv[1]);
      if ((n < 0) || (n > 15))
	{
	  perror ("Invalid range\n");
	  return 1;
	}

      mbp_set_intensity (n);
    }

  return 0;
}

```

Compile:
gcc -o mbp_nv_bl mbp_nv_bl.c

Get current intensity:
sudo ./mbp_nv_bl

Set intensity (0 - 15)
sudo ./mbp_nv_bl 10

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-20
> OK but can you clarify where loadbios and bios.bin + ivt.bin are necessary/optional/notusable.

Here are some of the findings:

For old 32-bit models with intel card, it can get the correct setting without bios, this is the simplest case.

For my mb, it also uses intel card. But it needs to scan the bios for video modes. If that fails, it prints a warning "Can't detect panel mode" and ends with a screen not found error.

For fglrx driver, it doesn't just scan the bios, but emulate a INT10 calls to real mode vbe function. This required the interrupt vector at 0x40 to be set correctly, this is what IVT.bin is for (Setting INT10 alone should be enough, but loading the whole table is easier as user don't need to know how to interpret the CS:IP value).

For nvidia driver, it doesn't need bios. But somehow, the backlight value is set too low. You can see if my tool can change it to normal. And btw, the backlight module scans DMI to identify hardware, so DMI issue could cause it to not loaded properly.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-20
> **pxwpxw said:**
> Works well, from 0 to 15. with the nvidia backlight module unloaded ( mbp_nvidia_bl ).
Where do you get those i/o addresses?

I will try a restart with it unloaded.
EDIT: restart for pc boot is ok, unchanged just no F1 F2 key control. Restart for efi boot is unchnged, still black.

I will do this tomorow.-
I can try run it in a startup script to see if it does some good. 
I can't see the console, so it has to be automated.

I think the cause of this problem is that nvidia store the default backlight value in some MMIO register, which is initialized properly by POST. While in efi mode, its value remains 0. When nvidia driver starts, it reads the register and set the backlight with i/o port. As it's the driver that changes the setting, you need to run the problem after X server starts, such as some xinit script.

And BTW, is backlight module loaded with loadbios ? backlight requires DMI, which should be all right with bios loaded (system bios at 0xF0000). You can use dmidecode to check DMI information:

sudo dmidecode

---

### Post by [LocK] on 2009-03-20
Hi,

I've been trying bean123's grub64.efi (from #430 post) on my MB3,1.

On first shot went:
```

loadbios (hd1,2)/bios.bin (hd1,2)/ivt.bin
linux  /boot/vmlinuz root=/dev/sda2 video=efifb

```
result of this you can see on attached screen1.jpg and it simply frozes on that screen.

Second try was more succesful:
```

loadbios (hd1,2)/bios.bin (hd1,2)/ivt.bin
linux  /boot/vmlinuz root=/dev/sda2 video=efifb **noefi**

```
now, system starts booting, but frame buffer was crappy (see screen2.jpg) but xorg starts and work fine.

i suppose that bug is connected with agp under efi, becouse on kernels without compiled intel_agp frame buffer works ok and also when i added agp=off option in to kernel parameter frame buffer was ok.

have you any ideas how to fix/workaround this problem ?

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-21

i suppose that bug is connected with agp under efi, becouse on kernels without compiled intel_agp frame buffer works ok and also when i added agp=off option in to kernel parameter frame buffer was ok.

It's possible to fix it by reinitialize video registers, but the process is hardware specific and not suitable for mainstream use. Anyway, what's important is that you can run X-Windows without the fb driver. If you want to login to console occasionally, just use frame buffer and the agp=off option.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-21
Hi,

I've uploaded the patch to grub-devel. Here are some notes:

The command loadbios is now renamed as loadrom, and remember to sync with latest svn before applying, as the patch rely on the new command interface.

Also, to generate the efi image with grub-mkimage, you should add module minicmd, and remove module boot.

1. I used "sudo dmidecode -t bios" to check for dmi. 

---------------------
dmidecode works for efi boot with loadbios /bios.bin, any level from root console runlevel S to runlevel 2 desktop.

dmidecode does not work for any grub64.efi boot without /bios.bin.
--------------------

2. I used mbp_nv_bl from post #438 to check backlight control.

Works fine for pc bios boot, in a script /etc/rc2.d/S99xblighter (after gdm). This worked for pcbios boot, effective at console login runlevel 2, subsequently overridden at desktop by nvidia mbp_nvidia_bl if present. 
pxw@mbp:~$ cat /etc/rc2.d/S99xblighter 
#! /bin/sh
# turn nvidia backlight up to max
/usr/local/bin/mbp_nv_bl 15 >/dev/null 2>&1


Also worked for pc boot, from command line at root console runlevel S in jaunty 2.6.28-amd64, with no nvidia modules or drivers, it changed the console screen brightness.

For efi boot mbp_nv_bl does not work anywhere, even when dmidecode is working, so cant fix the backlight that way - problem is deeper.

loading/unloading nvidia's mbp_nvidia_bl has no effect on efi boot.
----------------------

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-22
Hi pxwpxw,

Perhaps you can try save/load the whole mmio space. I've written a small tool to do so:

```

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <sys/io.h>
#include <sys/mman.h>
#include <sys/stat.h>

void
quit (char *msg)
{
  puts (msg);
  exit (1);
}

int main (int argc, char **argv)
{
  int hi, ho;
  int size;
  char *ptr;
  int is_load;
  struct stat st;
  int ret = 0;

  if (argc != 4)
    quit ("Usage:\n mmio_access {save | load} mmio_resouce file");

  is_load = (argv[1][0] == 'l');

  hi = open (argv[2], O_RDWR);
  if (hi < 0)
    quit ("open mmio fails");

  if (fstat (hi, &st) < 0)
    quit ("stat fails");

  ptr = mmap (0, st.st_size, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE, MAP_SHARED,
	      hi, 0);
  if (ptr == MAP_FAILED)
    quit ("mmap fails");

  if (is_load)
    {
      ho = open (argv[3], O_RDONLY);
      if (ho < 0)
	{
	  puts ("open input fails");
	  ret++;
	}
      else
	{
	  read (ho, ptr, st.st_size);
	  close (ho);
	}
    }
  else
    {
      ho = open (argv[3], O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
      if (ho < 0)
	{
	  puts ("open output fails");
	  ret++;
	}
      else
	{
	  write (ho, ptr, st.st_size);
	  close (ho);
	}
    }

  munmap (ptr, st.st_size);
  close (hi);

  return ret;
}

```

First, you need to know the mmio resource file, use:

lspci | grep VGA

Output is something like this:
00:02.0 VGA compatible controller: Intel Corporation Mobile GM965/GL960 Integrated Graphics Controller (rev 03)

Then display device is 00:02.0. From your previous screenshot, your device should be 01:00.0. You can use ls to list the pci node:

ls /sys/devices/pci0000:00/0000:01:00.0/

There should be files resourceN. resouce0 should be 1048576 long, this is your mmio file.

Save it in pc mode (in console mode):
sudo ./mmio_access save /sys/devices/pci0000:00/0000:01:00.0/resource0 mmio_dump

Load it in efi mode (in console mode):
sudo ./mmio_access load /sys/devices/pci0000:00/0000:01:00.0/resource0 mmio_dump

Then see if you can start x-windows properly.

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-22
Hi,

This new version of grub64.efi support loading mmio from grub2.

The linux command show some useful information which can be used to determine the mmio resource file:

Display controller: 0:2.0
MMIO (bar0): 0x50100000

The display controller line shows pci device number, and MMIO line shows the resource (bar0), so the corresponding mmio device is:

/sys/devices/pci0000:00/0000:00:02.0/resource0

The file size should be about 1M.

Then, boot to pc mode, use vga=9 option, which is mode 1024x768x32. Inside console mode, use mmio_access with the above file:

sudo mmio_access save /sys/devices/pci0000:00/0000:00:02.0/resource0 /mmio.bin

Then in grub2, use the following command to load it:

loadmmio - /mmio.bin

- means to use the address detected in the previous linux command (0x50100000 in my case), you can specify another address manually.

Please note that you should run loadmmio command as the last one, just before boot. Because after loadmmio, the screen would scramble, you need to type "boot" blindly. In console mode, it changes, but still unreadable, but it should be all right start X.org starts.

BTW, with loadmmio, when you exit X-windows and back to console mode, the screen is displayed normally at 1024x768, just like when you create the mmio dump in pc mode.

Hi,

This new mmio_access.c allow you to select an range:
```

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <sys/io.h>
#include <sys/mman.h>
#include <sys/stat.h>

void
quit (char *msg)
{
  puts (msg);
  exit (1);
}

int main (int argc, char **argv)
{
  int hi, ho;
  int size;
  char *ptr;
  int is_load, ofs, len;
  struct stat st;
  int ret = 0;

  if (argc < 4)
    quit ("Usage:\n mmio {save | load} mmio_resouce file [offset] [length]");

  is_load = (argv[1][0] == 'l');

  hi = open (argv[2], O_RDWR);
  if (hi < 0)
    quit ("open mmio fails");

  if (fstat (hi, &st) < 0)
    quit ("stat fails");

  ofs = (argc >= 5) ? strtoul (argv[4], 0, 0) : 0;
  len = (argc >= 6) ? strtoul (argv[5], 0, 0) : (st.st_size - ofs);

  ptr = mmap (0, len, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE, MAP_SHARED,
	      hi, ofs);
  if (ptr == MAP_FAILED)
    quit ("mmap fails");

  if (is_load)
    {
      ho = open (argv[3], O_RDONLY);
      if (ho < 0)
	{
	  puts ("open input fails");
	  ret++;
	}
      else
	{
	  read (ho, ptr, len);
	  close (ho);
	}
    }
  else
    {
      ho = open (argv[3], O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
      if (ho < 0)
	{
	  puts ("open output fails");
	  ret++;
	}
      else
	{
	  write (ho, ptr, len);
	  close (ho);
	}
    }

  munmap (ptr, len);
  close (hi);

  return ret;
}

```

Then use this command to capture mmio:

sudo mmio_access save /sys/devices/.../resource0 mmio1.bin 0x7e0000 0x20000

Then in grub, use this to load it:
loadmmio 0xd27e0000 /mmio1.bin

oh btw, vga=9 may not be 1024x768x32. You can use vga=ask and choose the right mode from the list.

---

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <sys/io.h>
#include <sys/mman.h>
#include <sys/stat.h>

void
quit (char *msg)
{
  puts (msg);
  exit (1);
}

int main (int argc, char **argv)
{
  int hi, ho;
  int size;
  char *ptr;
  int is_load, ofs, len;
  struct stat st;
  int ret = 0;

  if (argc < 4)
    quit ("Usage:\n mmio {save | load} mmio_resouce file [offset] [length]");

  is_load = (argv[1][0] == 'l');

  hi = open (argv[2], O_RDWR);
  if (hi < 0)
    quit ("open mmio fails");

  if (fstat (hi, &st) < 0)
    quit ("stat fails");

  ofs = (argc >= 5) ? strtoul (argv[4], 0, 0) : 0;
  len = (argc >= 6) ? strtoul (argv[5], 0, 0) : (st.st_size - ofs);

  ptr = mmap (0, len, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE, MAP_SHARED,
	      hi, ofs);
  if (ptr == MAP_FAILED)
    quit ("mmap fails");

  if (is_load)
    {
      ho = open (argv[3], O_RDONLY);
      if (ho < 0)
	{
	  puts ("open input fails");
	  ret++;
	}
      else
	{
	  read (ho, ptr, len);
	  close (ho);
	}
    }
  else
    {
      ho = open (argv[3], O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
      if (ho < 0)
	{
	  puts ("open output fails");
	  ret++;
	}
      else
	{
	  write (ho, ptr, len);
	  close (ho);
	}
    }

  munmap (ptr, len);
  close (hi);

  return ret;
}

```

sudo mmio_access save /sys/devices/.../resource0 mmio1.bin 0x7e0000 0x20000

Then in grub, use this to load it:
loadmmio 0xd27e0000 /mmio1.bin

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-23
> **pxwpxw said:**
> Yes looks like a dead end if you think we tried about everything with no sign of success. 
Sounds like the coding you had in mind would be well beyond me.
Anyway, from what I see here, the xorg "nv" driver is no better than the fbdev driver.  

I thought that since the nvidia proprietary driver on the MBP41 was clearly running the desktop, that if the problem was indeed backlight (as against some video level issue) then that would be external to the nvidia software and not quite so mysterious to track down, but I don't know how to attack that one.

Well, it's actually not very difficult to track down the backlight problem, but it needs to be done on your side. The function to enable backlight is in xf86-video-nv/src/nv_driver.c, NVBacklightEnable:

```

static void NVBacklightEnable(NVPtr pNv,  Bool on)
{
    /* This is done differently on each laptop.  Here we
       define the ones we know for sure. */

#if defined(__powerpc__)
    if((pNv->Chipset == 0x10DE0179) ||
       (pNv->Chipset == 0x10DE0189) ||
       (pNv->Chipset == 0x10DE0329))
    {
       /* NV17,18,34 Apple iMac, iBook, PowerBook */
      CARD32 tmp_pmc, tmp_pcrt;
      tmp_pmc = pNv->PMC[0x10F0/4] & 0x7FFFFFFF;
      tmp_pcrt = pNv->PCRTC0[0x081C/4] & 0xFFFFFFFC;
      if(on) {
          tmp_pmc |= (1 << 31);
          tmp_pcrt |= 0x1;
      }
      pNv->PMC[0x10F0/4] = tmp_pmc;
      pNv->PCRTC0[0x081C/4] = tmp_pcrt;
    }
#endif

    if(pNv->LVDS) {
       if(pNv->twoHeads) {
           if((pNv->Chipset & 0x0ff0) != 0x0110) {
               pNv->PMC[0x130C/4] = on ? 3 : 7;
           } else if(SUBVENDOR_ID(pNv->PciInfo) == 0x1028 &&
                     SUBDEVICE_ID(pNv->PciInfo) == 0xd4) {
               // Dell Inspiron 8200, GeForce2 Go
               CARD32 tmp_pcrt = pNv->PCRTC0[0x081C/4] & 0xFFFFFFFC;
               if(on)
                   tmp_pcrt |= 0x1;
               pNv->PCRTC0[0x081C/4] = tmp_pcrt;
           }
       }
    } else {
       CARD32 fpcontrol;

       fpcontrol = pNv->PRAMDAC[0x0848/4] & 0xCfffffCC;

       /* cut the TMDS output */
       if(on) fpcontrol |= pNv->fpSyncs;
       else fpcontrol |= 0x20000022;

       pNv->PRAMDAC[0x0848/4] = fpcontrol;
    }
}

```
 
There are several condition here, you need to find out which branch is executed by printing debug message using xf86DrvMsg. Then, you need to decide the value of pNv->PCRTC0 and pNv->PRAMDAC, which are in the MMIO. You can print the value of pNv->PCRTC0 and pNv->PRAMDAC, subtracted by pNv->IOAddress (MMIO base address), and you get an offset. The MMIO address of grub2 is 0xd2000000, adding the offset, the you know what register to set.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-03-24
svn rev 2043 patched loadrom.diff on imac81 x86_64 with ati fglrx.

I find loadrom needs time when called in menuentry, otherwise linux load fails or loadrom fails. So I had to use sleep.
Then it works very nicely with agp.
```

insmod sleep

menuentry "loadrom hd0,4 sda4" {
	search --set /ivt.bin
	loadrom /bios.bin /ivt.bin
	sleep 1
	root=hd0,4
	linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda4 video=vesafb
	initrd /initrd.img
}

```

This is not apparent from command line with manual entry pauses.
---------------------
Also posted on grub-devel list.

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-24
Hi,

I have discovered the cause for screen distortion for intel agp driver. It's in drivers/char/agp/intel-agp.c, inside intel_i965_driver structure:

```

static const struct agp_bridge_driver intel_i965_driver = {
        .owner                  = THIS_MODULE,
        .aperture_sizes         = intel_i830_sizes,
        .size_type              = FIXED_APER_SIZE,
        .num_aperture_sizes     = 4,
        .needs_scratch_page     = true,
        .configure              = intel_i915_configure,
        .fetch_size             = intel_i9xx_fetch_size,
        .cleanup                = intel_i915_cleanup,
        .tlb_flush              = intel_i810_tlbflush,
        .mask_memory            = intel_i965_mask_memory,
        .masks                  = intel_i810_masks,
        .agp_enable             = intel_i810_agp_enable,
        .cache_flush            = global_cache_flush,
        .create_gatt_table      = intel_i965_create_gatt_table,
        .free_gatt_table        = intel_i830_free_gatt_table,
        .insert_memory          = intel_i915_insert_entries,
        .remove_memory          = intel_i915_remove_entries,
        .alloc_by_type          = intel_i830_alloc_by_type,
        .free_by_type           = intel_i810_free_by_type,
        .agp_alloc_page         = agp_generic_alloc_page,
        .agp_destroy_page       = agp_generic_destroy_page,
        .agp_type_to_mask_type  = intel_i830_type_to_mask_type,
        .chipset_flush          = intel_i915_chipset_flush,
};

```

Remove .needs_scratch_page = true and recompile. The reason is that in bios mode, there is a 4K BIOS popup space, which doesn't exists in efi. Setting needs_scratch_page to true would mess up the gart table and therefore the screen. With this line gone, I can boot smoothly into console mode, although the x.org driver still needs to read bios, so loadbios is required as before.

Besides intel_i965_driver, intel_810_driver,intel_830_driver,intel_915_driver,intel_g33_driver all have needs_scratch_page set, others having such chipset can test if changing its value to false can solve the screen problem.

BTW, even without needs_scratch_page, bios mode boots ok.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-24
Hi,

I've found the real cause of this issue, it's actually caused by the wired gart layout in efi platform.

In pc mode, gart is a continuous block, frame buffer first, then 4K bios popup area.

```

Gart Offset          Physical Address
0x0000 - 0x3DFB  =>  0x3F000000 - 0x3FF7E000

```

But in efi mode, it's split into three blocks:

```

Gart Offset          Physical Address
0x0000 - 0x3DBF  =>  0x3F000000 - 0x3FF6F000
0x3DC0 - 0x3FFF  =>  Not used
0x4000 - 0x7DBF  =>  0x3F000000 - 0x3FF6F000
0x7DC0 - 0x7FFF  =>  Not used
0x8000 - 0x803F  =>  0x3FF70000 - 0x3FF7F000

```

The two range 0x0000 - 0x3DBF and 0x4000 - 0x7DBF mapped to the same physical address, changing either one of them would cause screen distortion. 0x8000 - 0x803F could be something similar to the bios popup area.

The gtt_entries member of agp_brige structure should be set to the number of gtt entries reserved. Linux calculates this value using aperture size plus the bios popup, this is correct for pc mode, but wrong for efi.

When needs_scratch_page is set, it initializes all the entries beyond gtt_entries to the scratch page address. In efi mode, all entries from 0x4000 is overwritten, this is the reason for screen distortion.

Therefore, setting needs_scratch_page to false doesn't really solve the problem, it just hides it. Entries from 0x4000 is still treated by system as free, so a latter agp allocation request could get the same range. To fix it properly, we need to increase the value of gtt_entries to include the whole area. In my case, we could do it like this:

```

    if (intel_private.gtt_entries == 3967)
        intel_private.gtt_entries = 8208;

```

3967 = 0x3DFC / 4 (pc mode reserved entries)
8208 = 0x8040 / 4 (efi mode reserved entries)

This wouldn't break pc, as there is no harm reserving more page than necessary.

Unfortunately, this patch has to be done in linux side, which needs to change the gtt_entries calculation algorithm to account for the strange gart layout in efi platform.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-25
Hi,

I've send a new patch to grub-devel. It cleanup the code a little bit, and move some stuff out of the linux loader.

There are two new module, loadbios and memrw.

loadbios contains the command fakebios and loadbios. fakebios is used to fake acpi and dmi information. loadbios allows you to load a video rom from external file. loadbios fakes acpi and dmi as well, so you don't need to call fakebios again.

memrw contains command write_dword. loadbios doesn't load IVT anymore, you need to get the INT10 address and write to memory address 0x40 using write_dword.

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-25
Hi,

The console screen problem is actually easier to fix than I thought. It turns out that efi uses pipeline B, which corresponds to the GART mapping at 0x4000. The mapping is controlled by video register DSPBSURF. When I set its value to 0, it uses GART at offset 0, and therefore no conflict with agp allocation.

This new grub.efi add a command fix_video, which patch video register mentioned above. The operation is card specific, so I need to check for graphic card before doing anything. Currently it only support Intel 965GM, if you see the "Unknown graphic card" error, please report the device id on screen.

With fix_video, I can boot to console mode with agp enabled. X.org driver may still need vbios and INT10 vector. There is a small catch though. If you go back to console mode from X-Window, the screen would have problem. This is because efi only use pipeline B, pipeline A is not initialized. When X-windows exits, it just resume output at pipeline A, therefore causing the problem.

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-25
BTW, for intel graphic card 910 - 965, video register DSPBSURF is at fixed location. You can try modifying it manually.

First, you should run linux with video=efifb option to see the PCI configuration, it should look something like this:

MMIO(0): 0x50100000
VMEM(2): 0x40000000

The mmio base is 0x50100000, adding DSPBSURF offset 0x7119C, we have 0x5017119C, we read the original value first:
```

read_dword 0x5017119C

```

The value should be 0x1000000, if it's not, stop right now.

Use write_dword to change its value to 0:
```

write_dword 0x5017119C 0

```

Read it back, its value should be 0 now:
```

read_dword 0x5017119C

```

Then, continue the loading of linux. If all goes well, you should be able to boot to console mode with agp enabled.

Oh BTW, from the x.org source code, I think the video register offset for radeonhd could be one of these:

D1GRPH_PRIMARY_SURFACE_ADDRESS = 0x6110,
D1GRPH_SECONDARY_SURFACE_ADDRESS = 0x6118,
D2GRPH_PRIMARY_SURFACE_ADDRESS = 0x6910,

pxwpxw, could you check it out ?

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-26
Hi,

This is another update, it contains the following changes with regards to the previous one:

1, Move loadbios command to commands/efi directory.
2, loadbios support an optional second parameter which is the int10 vector dump file.
3, The linux command enables frame buffer by default, so you don't need video= option anymore.
4. New command fix_video to fix video register issue with efi. With this, you can boot to console mode with agp enabled.
5, New shell script grub-dumpbios which can be used to generate the bios dump from pc mode.

As the commands usage have changed in the previous patches, here is a summary of what you should do with this latest patch:

1, Boot without bios dump.

This is the commands to use:

search --set /vmlinuz
fix_video
fakebios
linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sdb1
initrd /initrd.img

2, Boot with bios dump.

First, boot to pc mode, then use script grub-dumpbios to generate the dump files:

sudo grub-dumpbios -o /boot/

video bios dump is in vbios.bin, int10 vector is in int10.bin. /boot/ is the output directory.

Then, use the following commands in grub2:
search --set /vmlinuz
fix_video
loadbios /boot/vbios.bin /boot/int10.bin
linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sdb1
initrd /initrd.img

Moreover, if you're booting 2.6.27-28, amd64 kernel, you need to add noefi option.

The source has been uploaded to grub-devel

---

Oh nice, I'd add your card to the patch list then.

And now it seems console display problem only affects integral intel cards, for other graphic cards, fix_video command is not needed.
 
BTW, I just saw a strange nvidia config option:

Option "UseInt10Module" "boolean"

Enable use of the X Int10 module to soft-boot all secondary cards, rather than POSTing the cards through the NVIDIA kernel module. Default: off (POSTing is done through the NVIDIA kernel module).

Perhaps you can try setting it to true and see what happens. Don't forget to load vbios.bin and int10.bin for int10 support.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-27
> **pxwpxw said:**
> I suspect that for the others - MBP nvidia and IMac radeon, there is still a problem when getting out of desktop with CtrlAltF1  to console, getting blank screen  or crazy screen. 
So may be the same basic problem with common use of the intel_agp and agpgart and similar solution, IDK.

But I have to recheck all this.

Yeah, I believe so. For example, your imac81:

read_dword 0xd0526110
0x10000

You could try changing it to 0.

The problem with intel card is that the driver has needs_scratch_page set, so that it initialize gart in console mode, resulting in the crazy screen. For other cards, the display range is also invalid, but it doesn't touch agp until after X starts. The result is similar to the previous needs_scratch_page quick fix in #485.

---

-----------

read_dword 0xd0526110
10000

after boot from this menuentry there was no text. (with or without noefi)

menuentry "loadbios hd0,4 sda4" {
	root=hd0,4
	fix_video
	loadbios /boot/vbios.bin /boot/int10.bin
	sleep 1
	root=hd0,4

	initrd /initrd.img

When the desktop ran, exit from desktop to console was a black screen, same as before.

That was D1GRPH_PRIMARY_SURFACE_ADDRESS = 0x6110,

I looked at the xorg radeon driver rhd_regs.h and tried reading a few others and found all  0 or ffffffff.

It would be nice to solve that one, but we can get full console access with alternative menuentry to boot using video=efifb and the xorg  Driver "fbdev". Or boot 'single' to root console with video=efifb. Or boot into runlevel 2 without automatic gui login.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-03-28
> **cyberdork33 said:**
> I am not getting anywhere on iMac5,1 (sorry I've been gone).

Trying to load the ivt.bin just gives an error "Invalid int10 dump size"

and no boot (it just hangs)

Also trying to boot how I did before works, but I have a non-working keyboard again. (w/ video=efifb acpi=force noefi)

The commands usage change a little bit.

First, it doesn't support the whole ivt.bin, only int10.bin. In fact, you don't need to worry about the details if you use grub-dumpbios to generate the files:

sudo grub-dumpbios -o /boot/

And in grub2:

loadbios /boot/vbios.bin /boot/int10.bin

Also, if you have intel graphic card, you could try the new fix_video command and fix the screen distortion problem in console mode.

> **bean123 said:**
> This new grub.efi add video fix for Intel 945GM, thanks pxwpxw for the testing.

grub32.efi #523 0n MacBook21 booting Ubuntu810 2.6.27 x86.
All goes well, agp with efi boot or pc-bios boot has same performance.

Result is only for ubuntu810 x86
Linux mb21 2.6.27-7-generic #1 SMP Fri Oct 24 06:42:44 UTC 2008 i686 GNU/

lsmod | grep agp
intel_agp              33724  1 
agpgart                42184  3 drm,intel_agp

lspci -nn | grep 945
00:00.0 Host bridge [0600]: Intel Corporation Mobile 945GM/PM/GMS, 943/940GML
 and 945GT Express Memory Controller Hub [8086:27a0] (rev 03)
00:02.0 VGA compatible controller [0300]: Intel Corporation Mobile 945GM/GMS,
 943/940GML Express Integrated Graphics Controller [8086:27a2] (rev 03)
00:02.1 Display controller [0380]: Intel Corporation Mobile 945GM/GMS/GME, 
 943/940GML Express Integrated Graphics Controller [8086:27a6] (rev 03)
-------------------------
Setting up-

No blacklist. 
No fbdev, just the xorg intel driver, with agp enabled.
Not required here -  grub-dumpbios (vbios.bin int10.bin) or loadbios
Required - fix_video (fix the console screen to enable agp)
Required - video=efifb to get console screen text.

grub.cfg 
```

menuentry "grub523 sda3" {
 root=hd0,3
 fix_video
 linux  /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda3 video=efifb
 initrd  /initrd.img
}

```

```

#/etc/X11/xorg.conf
# same for grub32.efi and grub-pc boot
Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
	Driver	"intel"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"

```

---

### Post by cyberdork33 on 2009-03-28
> **bean123 said:**
> The commands usage change a little bit.

First, it doesn't support the whole ivt.bin, only int10.bin. In fact, you don't need to worry about the details if you use grub-dumpbios to generate the files:

sudo grub-dumpbios -o /boot/

And in grub2:

loadbios /boot/vbios.bin /boot/int10.bin

Also, if you have intel graphic card, you could try the new fix_video command and fix the screen distortion problem in console mode.
OK thanks. I will try to give this a shot today.

I have a Radeon X1600.

I can see console on startup using video=efifb or vesafb

```
cyberdork33@Grover-Ubuntu:~$ uname -a
Linux Grover-Ubuntu 2.6.28-11-generic #38-Ubuntu SMP Fri Mar 27 09:00:52 UTC 2009 i686 GNU/Linux
cyberdork33@Grover-Ubuntu:~$ cat /proc/cmdline 
BOOT_IMAGE=/vmlinuz root=/dev/sda3 video=vesafb acpi=force
cyberdork33@Grover-Ubuntu:~$ lspci -nn |grep VGA
01:00.0 VGA compatible controller [0300]: ATI Technologies Inc M56P [Radeon Mobility X1600] [1002:71c5]
```

That's odd, I wonder why loadbios can't find the SMBIOS address.

Anyway, a quick fix is to load system bios as well, so that DMI signature can be found:

sudo dd if=/dev/mem of=/boot/fullbios.bin bs=65536 skip=12 count=4

And in grub2:

loadbios /boot/fullbios.bin /boot/int10.bin

### Post by bean123 on 2009-04-03
> **cputoaster said:**
> I tried, and get this before it stops:

```

[Linux....
Video mode: 1280x800-32@0
Display controller: 2:0:0
Device id: 87010de
MMIO(0): 0x92000000
VMEM(1): 0x80000000
VMEM(3): 0x90000000
Frame buffer base: 0x80010000
Video line length: 8192
[Initrd....

```

Yeah, you should add the fakebios command to mimic bios data.

BTW, there are some linux command line option you can try. If you're booting 2.6.27-28 amd64 kernel, you need to add noefi option, also, use video=efifb to show console message. Sometimes you need acpi=force as well.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-04-08
> **D4T said:**
> Yeah thats with acpi=force and pretty much every other option listed here or that I can think of.



Some points to check -
The  grub-efi binary attached at post #523 is still the latest, not all the fixes are found on SVN yet.

There was a Xserve1,1 case started at post #333 and subsequently working, although initially with different issues.  You might check the thread after there, to see if anything got lost in the tidying up.

You should probably be using options  'fakebios' and for 2.6.28 64bit kernel 'noefi'. Maybe 'acpi=force' as well.  
I dont think you can use 'loadbios' since you need to dumpbios from bios boot - NA on Xserve.

Can't think of anything else.

### Post by D4T on 2009-04-09
Ok a couple of things to note regarding Xserve hardware:

Xserve 1,1 has 32bit EFI and appears to only boot 32bit kernel's. 

Xserve 1,2 has 64bit EFI and can only boot 64 bit kernel's.

In both cases if you boot the wrong kernel version you end up with lots of IRQ errors as per my previous photos.

Not sure where the limitation is here? Maybe someone with more knowledge then myself can weigh in?

---

> **pxwpxw said:**
> That is a new one I think, no such 64/32 bit kernel  restriction for MBP, IMAC, MacBook that I know of.

Trying to see where SVN caught up, last mention  was post #507 Bean said source uploaded (round 25 March 2009).

But there is current discussion to get from these Apple specific 'hacks' to the full grub code in best way.

I will have another look at grub-devel, but I think we have to ask Bean what SVN version to match #523 binaries for Xserve in particular. Last compile I tried was r 2043, now history (post #484), I am just using #523 binary for grub64 and grub32, for i386 and amd64 kernels.

Bean?

Hi,

r2063 is the version I commit fakebios patch, but then there is discussion about the split of modules and it got reversed. So if you want to compile it yourself, use r2063.

Also, it's the version that you need to use fakebios command explicitly, in other versions, the faking of bios data is run automatically by the linux loader.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-04-09
> **pxwpxw said:**
> Hi,
We have been using 'fakebios' with your #523 binaries, do you mean there is another SVN version where that is run automatically by the linux loader?
If so, what is the version please?

The faking of bios data is added a while a ago to solve the ACPI problem. SVN r2063 and #523 split the function from linux loader and place in a standalone module loadbios, so you need to run fakebios manually. In other versions, that function is run automatically. In fact, you can tell it by the "ACPI: xxxx" information line just before linux starts.

---

### Post by apostille on 2009-04-10
The Xserve does not have BIOS compatibility, and so you'll need to boot through EFI. The Apple EFI implementation will not boot directly off ISO9660 media, so you'll need to find a way to get to the EFI console. You'll need a kernel configured with EFI support. There's no especially sensible way to get a working framebuffer on this hardware, so you'll need to work out what arguments need to be passed to your distribution's installer in order to get it to run over the serial port. elilo or grub-efi should be able to boot the kernel and initramfs. 

I've been catologing my research at.

---

There's no especially sensible way to get a working framebuffer on this hardware, so you'll need to work out what arguments need to be passed to your distribution's installer in order to get it to run over the serial port. elilo or grub-efi should be able to boot the kernel and initramfs. 

I've been catologing my research at.

Why is there no sensible way to get a working framebuffer ? D4T has fb detected properly. Perhaps you can try grub.efi and see if it works.

# Created by D4T

timeout=5

	echo "first sleep"
	sleep 5
	echo "running fakebios"
	fakebios
	echo " second sleep"
	sleep 5
	echo "setting search"
	search --set /vmlinuz

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-04-16
This grub.cfg shows some tests I ran on IMAC81 for grub64.efi r 2074,
with Xserve people in mind.

Referring to each menuentry

0- fakebios test shows results obtained this way change for second run, depending on time and activity, and different for MBP41 and MacBook21. I think the result may be misleading compared with  actual use of fakebios in a real boot sequence.
(pictures fakebios1 first run, fakebios2 after 30 seconds)

1- fakebios and noefi used to boot debian sid (unstable) amd64, the same format used for all 64bit kernels from 2.6.26 to 2.6.29

2- booting from installer cd.

3- booting from Network boot installer images (quick test of bootability, and easy install)

[http://www.debian.org/distrib/netinst](http://www.debian.org/distrib/netinst)
down at the end -
[http://ftp.nl.debian.org/debian/dists/lenny/main/installer-amd64/current/images/netboot/debian-installer/amd64/](http://ftp.nl.debian.org/debian/dists/lenny/main/installer-amd64/current/images/netboot/debian-installer/amd64/)

I don't have the IPMI problem here so can't comment on that.

grub.cfg

```

# grub.cfg pxw 20090316

timeout=10
default=0

set F1=ctrl-x
set F2=ctrl-c

menuentry "0-testfakebios" {
# for debugging set debug=efi
	hexdump -s 0xc0000 (mem)
	fakebios
	hexdump -s 0xc0000 (mem)
# deliberate error to get wait for key
	xxx
}
# 2.6.29 kernel
menuentry "1-debian sid x86_64 fbdev sda9" {
	fakebios
	root=hd0,9
	linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda9 video=efifb noefi
	initrd /initrd.img
}
# 2.6.28 CD installer
menuentry "2-ubuntu x86_64 alternate installer cd" {
	fakebios
	search --set /install/vmlinuz
	linux /install/vmlinuz video=efiifb noefi
	initrd /install/initrd.gz
}
# 2.6.26 net installer
menuentry "3-debian lenny64 net install images" {
	fakebios
	search --set /lenny64/linux
	linux /lenny64/linux video=efiifb
	initrd /lenny64/initrd.gz
}
menuentry "REBOOT" {
	reboot
}

```

### Post by bean123 on 2009-04-16
> **D4T said:**
> Right, using pxwpxw's example menuentry for running hexdump before and after fakebios here are my results.

This is the output from an Xserve 1,1:

[[IMG]http://img27.imageshack.us/img27/8915/img0075ksi.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img27.imageshack.us/my.php?image=img0075ksi.jpg)

This is the output from a MacBook Pro 1,2:

[[IMG]http://img26.imageshack.us/img26/5904/img0076ikg.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img26.imageshack.us/my.php?image=img0076ikg.jpg)

This is the output from a MacBook Pro 5,1 (note the mirror like glossy screen LOL):

[[IMG]http://img21.imageshack.us/img21/2373/img0077f.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img21.imageshack.us/my.php?image=img0077f.jpg)

They are very mixed indeed and I'm hoping they will help.

Oh, I belive I've found the cause, it's quite silly actually. In commands/efi/loadbios.c, add a line "return 0;" at the end of grub_cmd_fakebios.

```

static grub_err_t
grub_cmd_fakebios (struct grub_command *cmd __attribute__ ((unused)),
		   int argc __attribute__ ((unused)),
		   char *argv[] __attribute__ ((unused)))
{
  if (enable_rom_area ())
    {
      fake_bios_data (1);
      lock_rom_area ();
    }
  else
    fake_bios_data (0);

  return 0;   // <- add this line
}

```

---

### Post by D4T on 2009-04-16
> **bean123 said:**
> Oh, I belive I've found the cause, it's quite silly actually. In commands/efi/loadbios.c, add a line "return 0;" at the end of grub_cmd_fakebios.

```

static grub_err_t
grub_cmd_fakebios (struct grub_command *cmd __attribute__ ((unused)),
		   int argc __attribute__ ((unused)),
		   char *argv[] __attribute__ ((unused)))
{
  if (enable_rom_area ())
    {
      fake_bios_data (1);
      lock_rom_area ();
    }
  else
    fake_bios_data (0);

  return 0;   // <- add this line
}

```

Aha, will give it a go and let you know.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-04-16
> **pxwpxw said:**
> The hexdump was showing all 00, which is seen as ROM image present?

Oh, I use 0xffffffff as an indicator that video rom is not empty, perhaps should check all zero as well, in commands/efi/loadbios.c:

```

static int
enable_rom_area (void)
{
  grub_pci_address_t addr;
  grub_uint32_t *rom_ptr;

  rom_ptr = (grub_uint32_t *) VBIOS_ADDR;
  if ((*rom_ptr != BLANK_MEM) && (*rom_ptr != 0)) // change this line
    {
      grub_printf ("ROM image present.\n");
      return 0;
    }
...

```

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-04-16
> **bean123 said:**
> Oh, I use 0xffffffff as an indicator that video rom is not empty, perhaps should check all zero as well, in commands/efi/loadbios.c:

```

static int
enable_rom_area (void)
{
  grub_pci_address_t addr;
  grub_uint32_t *rom_ptr;

  rom_ptr = (grub_uint32_t *) VBIOS_ADDR;
  if ((*rom_ptr != BLANK_MEM) && (*rom_ptr != 0)) // change this line
    {
      grub_printf ("ROM image present.\n");
      return 0;
    }
...

```

Yep, thats what I was looking at.

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-04-16
Oh BTW, perhaps Xserve doesn't protect the video rom area. To check it, you can use:

write_dword 0xc0000 0x1234

Then read it back with:

read_dword 0xc0000

If the result is 0x1234, then it means address 0xc0000 can be written freely.

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-04-17
> **D4T said:**
> To disable IPMI support (or LOM as you are likely to know it is) you need to compile a custom kernel with all IPMI options turned off.


Just noting that on ubuntu810 IPMI options are all loadable modules
```

pxw@im81:~$ grep IPMI /boot/config-2.6.27-11-generic 
CONFIG_IPMI_DEVICE_INTERFACE=m
CONFIG_IPMI_HANDLER=m
# CONFIG_IPMI_PANIC_EVENT is not set
CONFIG_IPMI_POWEROFF=m
CONFIG_IPMI_SI=m
CONFIG_IPMI_WATCHDOG=m

```
Maybe they can just be unloaded or blacklisted.

---

So I think something is wrong somewhere.

i boot with something like:

search --set /vmlinuz
fakebios
linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda3 video=efifb
initrd /initrd.img

the linux partition is the third one ... (hd0,3) in the new grub2 notation, so i guess /dev/sda3 is correct

i tried adding acpi=force and agp=off... and even giving the full path to the vmlinuz and initrd (i've actually never booted yet into this system... freshly installed, is a ubuntu jaunty rc 64bit by the way)

hovewer it always get stuck after printing this line:

[initrd, addr=0x7f85a000, size=0x795370]

do you have some suggestion?


thank you

If you are booting from the grub> command line you need to do 
grub> boot

for jaunty amd64, you may need 
 linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda3 video=efifb noefi agp=off 

Also if you do see some garbage screen after boot, it may need a line before linux -

 fix_video

I am not sure what is needed for the MB31, depends partly on the video card (intel or otherwise).

And importantly, what version of grub64.efi have you got?

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-04-22
> **TBD said:**
> 
3,1 has the intel x3100
did someone manage to avoid disabling graphics with grub-efi? i can't live without compiz's scale plugin :D

If you run 'fix_video' from the grub> command line, it should return some brief information about your x3100 graphics card and may enable booting into the intel driver.
Also in ubuntu, for your graphics card id.
```

$ lspci -nn | grep VGA

```
Please post both results for the record.

> 
i used the build 2074 that was posted in this topic :)

Thats good.

> 
now i only need to figure out how to make refit boot grub-efi by default  (edit: too bad, it can't be done :/ )

By blessing grub.efi instead of rEFIt, you can boot grub.efi without refit.
Then you boot OSX or linux from the grub.cfg menu. 

In OSX, terminal for example -
(See man bless)

```

$ sudo find / -name grub64.efi
/efi/grub2074/grub64.efi
$ sudo bless --folder /  --file /efi/grub2074/grub64.efi
$ bless --info

```

That will work until you boot into OSX. Every time you boot to OSX, the rEFitBlesser  will reset the bless to OSX and rEFIt. That is a safeguard for OSX if you use hibernate, so that restart returns to OSX. So disabling it can be a hazard - its your risk. (more explanation on rEFIt web page)

By blessing grub.efi instead of rEFIt, you can boot grub.efi without refit.
Then you boot OSX or linux from the grub.cfg menu. 

In OSX, terminal for example -
(See man bless)

```

$ sudo find / -name grub64.efi
/efi/grub2074/grub64.efi
$ sudo bless --folder /  --file /efi/grub2074/grub64.efi
$ bless --info

```

That will work until you boot into OSX. Every time you boot to OSX, the rEFitBlesser  will reset the bless to OSX and rEFIt. That is a safeguard for OSX if you use hibernate, so that restart returns to OSX. So disabling it can be a hazard - its your risk. (more explanation on rEFIt web page)
