### Post by decoder_oh on 2009-04-23
> **pxwpxw said:**
> Good, apparently you located a specific Xserve grub64 big memory bug.
I think we have to leave that for Bean to look at.


Here is a fix for the problem, apply the patch against svn revision 2121 and it should work. The modifications where kindly provided by phcoder from the grub people. He said that this is a quick hack and a proper solution will follow as soon as he has time :) But these modifications worked for me and booted my Xserve 2,1 with 10 GB RAM :)

Thanks to him and all of you for your help :)


> 
Index: kern/efi/mm.c                                                                                                                                                             
===================================================================                                                                                                              
--- kern/efi/mm.c       (revision 2121)                                                                                                                                          
+++ kern/efi/mm.c       (working copy)                                                                                                                                           
@@ -59,7 +59,7 @@                                                                                                                                                                
   grub_efi_status_t status;                                                                                                                                                     
   grub_efi_boot_services_t *b;                                                                                                                                                  
                                                                                                                                                                                 
-#if GRUB_TARGET_SIZEOF_VOID_P < 8                                                                                                                                               
+#if 1                                                                                                                                                                           
   /* Limit the memory access to less than 4GB for 32-bit platforms.  */                                                                                                         
   if (address > 0xffffffff)                                                                                                                                                     
     return 0;                                                                                                                                                                   
@@ -218,7 +218,7 @@                                                                                                                                                              
        desc = NEXT_MEMORY_DESCRIPTOR (desc, desc_size))                                                                                                                         
     {                                                                                                                                                                           
       if (desc->type == GRUB_EFI_CONVENTIONAL_MEMORY                                                                                                                            
-#if GRUB_TARGET_SIZEOF_VOID_P < 8                                                                                                                                               
+#if 1                                                                                                                                                                           
          && desc->physical_start <= 0xffffffff                                                                                                                                  
 #endif                                                                                                                                                                          
          && desc->physical_start + PAGES_TO_BYTES (desc->num_pages) > 0x100000                                                                                                  
@@ -234,7 +234,7 @@                                                                                                                                                              
              desc->physical_start = 0x100000;                                                                                                                                   
            }

-#if GRUB_TARGET_SIZEOF_VOID_P < 8
+#if 1
          if (BYTES_TO_PAGES (filtered_desc->physical_start)
              + filtered_desc->num_pages
              > BYTES_TO_PAGES (0x100000000LL))
Index: loader/i386/efi/linux.c
===================================================================
--- loader/i386/efi/linux.c     (revision 2121)
+++ loader/i386/efi/linux.c     (working copy)
@@ -418,9 +418,6 @@
   /* Hardware interrupts are not safe any longer.  */
   asm volatile ("cli" : : );

-  /* Load the IDT and the GDT for the bootstrap.  */
-  asm volatile ("lidt %0" : : "m" (idt_desc));
-  asm volatile ("lgdt %0" : : "m" (gdt_desc));

 #ifdef __x86_64__

@@ -430,10 +427,19 @@
   asm volatile ( "mov %0, %%rbx" : : "m" (params->code32_start));
   asm volatile ( "mov %0, %%rsi" : : "m" (real_mode_mem));

+  /* Load the IDT and the GDT for the bootstrap.  */
+  asm volatile ("lidt %0" : : "m" (idt_desc));
+  asm volatile ("lgdt %0" : : "m" (gdt_desc));
+
   asm volatile ( "ljmp *%0" : : "m" (jumpvector));

 #else

+  /* Load the IDT and the GDT for the bootstrap.  */
+  asm volatile ("lidt %0" : : "m" (idt_desc));
+  asm volatile ("lgdt %0" : : "m" (gdt_desc));
+
+
   /* Pass parameters.  */
   asm volatile ("movl %0, %%ecx" : : "m" (params->code32_start));
   asm volatile ("movl %0, %%esi" : : "m" (real_mode_mem));


---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-04-23
> **decoder_oh said:**
> Here is a fix for the problem, apply the patch against svn revision 2121 and it should work. The modifications where kindly provided by phcoder from the grub people. He said that this is a quick hack and a proper solution will follow as soon as he has time :) But these modifications worked for me and booted my Xserve 2,1 with 10 GB RAM :)

Thanks to him and all of you for your help :)

Thanks for that info, I just saw your  grub-devel post. 

Were you able to, or could you,  check which of the 2 patches did the trick?

EDIT: copy of decoder_oh modified patch 
```

Index: kern/efi/mm.c
================================================== =================
--- kern/efi/mm.c (revision 2121)
+++ kern/efi/mm.c (working copy)
@@ -59,7 +59,7 @@
grub_efi_status_t status;
grub_efi_boot_services_t *b;

-#if GRUB_TARGET_SIZEOF_VOID_P < 8
+#if 1
/* Limit the memory access to less than 4GB for 32-bit platforms. */
if (address > 0xffffffff)
return 0;
@@ -218,7 +218,7 @@
desc = NEXT_MEMORY_DESCRIPTOR (desc, desc_size))
{
if (desc->type == GRUB_EFI_CONVENTIONAL_MEMORY
-#if GRUB_TARGET_SIZEOF_VOID_P < 8
+#if 1
&& desc->physical_start <= 0xffffffff
#endif
&& desc->physical_start + PAGES_TO_BYTES (desc->num_pages) > 0x100000
@@ -234,7 +234,7 @@
desc->physical_start = 0x100000;
}

-#if GRUB_TARGET_SIZEOF_VOID_P < 8
+#if 1
if (BYTES_TO_PAGES (filtered_desc->physical_start) + filtered_desc->num_pages > BYTES_TO_PAGES (0x100000000LL))

```

### Post by bean123 on 2009-04-24
> **decoder_oh said:**
> Ok I'll see if I find some time to post either here or in the wiki what I used. However, I still have some unsolved problems there. For example we have a RAID controller in our Xserve which seems to be rebranded by Apple but I don't know yet exactly what it is and how to use it.


Edit: I should note that there is no way around it, as the RAID card "replaces" the regular backplane for SATA/SAS. No way to access any of the hard disks :(

If it's hardware raid, then you don't need to access individual disks, all disks are treated as a single raid device. However, if linux doesn't recognize it, then you have to disable hardware raid and use soft raid provided by linux. grub is capable of booting from soft raid level 0/1/4/5/6/10.

### Post by bean123 on 2009-04-25
> **pxwpxw said:**
> I tried grub crc (have to add the crc module) and got ffffffff for all the  7 /vmlinuz and /boot/vmlinuz-.... on my HD.
It returned sensible 31 bit hex for all the initrd.
I tried to cross check with linux cksum, but it seems to be using a different polynomial. It gave sensible 32bit results for both vmlinuz and initrd.
So I think something is wrong somewhere.

I do a little checking today, the checksum of vmlinuz is indeed ffffffff, I guess this is intentional so that it can detect corrupted image. BTW, the result of cksum is not the same as crc32, a simple way to calculate crc32 is to compress the file with zip, then use unzip to list the file information:

```

unzip -lv aa.zip

```

It contains a column called "CRC-32".

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-04-25
> **bean123 said:**
> I do a little checking today, the checksum of vmlinuz is indeed ffffffff, I guess this is intentional so that it can detect corrupted image. BTW, the result of cksum is not the same as crc32, a simple way to calculate crc32 is to compress the file with zip, then use unzip to list the file information:

```

unzip -lv aa.zip

```

It contains a column called "CRC-32".

Thanks
EDIT: and I confirmed grub crc and linux unzip CRC agree for all vmlinuz and initrd.img

### Post by TBD on 2009-04-25
here's an update on the driver issue:

by removing agp=off i've been able to load the system with the intel drivers (instead of fbdev) but 3d acceleration doesn't work...

i tried adding/removing the fix_video line and the linux option video=efifb but nothing changed (apart from screen glitches during boot/shutdown time)

today i removed the ssd to boot into bios-compatibility mode and make a bios dump, then i added "loadbios /boot/vbios.bin /boot/int10.bin" on the grub.cfg, but again: nothing changed (and i tried even with/without fix_video and video=efifb)


> **pxwpxw said:**
> If you run 'fix_video' from the grub> command line, it should return some brief information about your x3100 graphics card and may enable booting into the intel driver.
Also in ubuntu, for your graphics card id.
```

$ lspci -nn | grep VGA

```
Please post both results for the record.



fix_video says it's a intel 965GM

lspci output is:

00:02.0 VGA compatible controller [0300]: Intel Corporation Mobile GM965/GL960 Integrated Graphics Controller [8086:2a02] (rev 03)


> **pxwpxw said:**
> 
Question about the OCZ vertex - how is it installed - is it a direct replacement for the MB31 HD connected internally? Is it really as fast as advertised?

yes, i replaced the internal 80GB 5400rpm drive with the vertex 30GB drive...

ssd haven't been around for much time, so i can't guarantee on their reliability... but performance wise they are very good...

ocz states these specs for their 30GB vertex:

30GB Max Performance**       
	Read: Up to 230 MB/s       
	Write: Up to 135MB/s       
	Sustained Write: Up to 80MB/s 

i tested read speed:

dario@macbook:~$ time sudo dd if=/dev/sda of=/dev/null bs=1M count=300
300+0 record dentro
300+0 record fuori
314572800 byte (315 MB) copiati, 2,3701 s, **133 MB/s**

real	0m2.390s
user	0m0.004s
sys	0m0.628s
dario@macbook:~$ 

as you can see it doesn't reach those numbers... but keep in mind that empty ssd are faster than used ones (but that's mostly true for write speed, read speed is less affected), and that the vertex's a sata2 drive... while afaik the macbook3,1 only has a sata1 controller (half the bandwidth)

then there's the advantage of ssd's random read/writes over normal hd... so overall this is a nice improvement to the previous 5400rpm drive :D

the boot time e.g. is noticeably faster (tough i haven't yet made any measure... but i planned to do a video and a bootchart)

then there're also added bonuses such as the fact it's completely silent... but certainly they aren't yet cheap nor spacious

00:02.0 VGA compatible controller [0300]: Intel Corporation Mobile GM965/GL960 Integrated Graphics Controller [8086:2a02] (rev 03)



Thanks for the update.

The fix_video is only to prevent interference to the console when booting with agp, that seems to happen only with intlel graphics. It seems to be detecing your GM965 correctly.
Other reports of intel 945/965 have not commented on using 3d acceleration. 
On a MacBook2,1 with gruub32.efi and with 945GM and modules agpgart intel_agp,  glxgears reports 1000 fps . The same result for pc-bios boot, or efi boot, using xorg driver intel. I have not tried compiz.
But that is for ubuntu810-i386. It will not run agp on ubuntu904-amd64, only fbdev.
Back at post #489, bean123 has a intel 965.


> 
yes, i replaced the internal 80GB 5400rpm drive with the vertex 30GB drive...

ssd haven't been around for much time, so i can't guarantee on their reliability... but performance wise they are very good...



The SSD info is interesting, no indication why it needs booting from grub.efi. Maybe its something to do with the partitioning system, grub-pc depends on MBR partition information at the first sector. 

OSX and grub.efi just uses GPT partitioning tables.

From grub.efi command line you can hexdump the MBR sector to see if there is a msdos partition table and boot-code.
```

grub> hexdump -n 512 (hd0)

```

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=twNkTkdCy84](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=twNkTkdCy84)

> 
OSX and grub.efi just uses GPT partitioning tables.

From grub.efi command line you can hexdump the MBR sector to see if there is a msdos partition table and boot-code.
```

grub> hexdump -n 512 (hd0)

```

i'll post it after next reboot :)

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using

It's normal, some intel card doesn't need bios dump at all. In my system, the intel driver won't start without bios dump as it need to scan video rom to locate the DDC table.

Yes I wish there was a way to dump grub text.

That looks like a correct MBR with grub-pc bootcode and partition table with 200MB efi fat32 and 2 x 15GB partitions. A check with refit partiton tool would confirm. I would expect it to boot for a hard drive, using grub-pc. 

If it was a usb external drive that would stop it booting with grub-pc on a Mac, but I assume it uses the same interface as the sata.

Anyway, you established that it boots with grub.efi.
 
From the OCZ forum posts the problems are not just for Apple Mac boxes, but you could start a new thread here for SSD on Macs if you would like to chase the SSD issue with other mac users here, but it's getting a bit off-topic now for this grub-efi thread.

It seems to be an issue with grub2-pc and SSD drives.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-04-27
> **TBD said:**
> mhn, all these informations... couldn't be added to the community wiki?

i could try doing it myself... but never done that before, i guess the forums account isn't fit and have to create another one? i'll start reading the [guidelines](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/MactelSupportTeam)

excuse me... how did you read the hexdump? which software have you used?


I just use a calculator with hex and decimal.

The partition table has 16bytes for each of 4 partitons, start and size in 512byte sectors, little-endian.
first partition in this example has start [COLOR="Red"]0x00000001[/COLOR], size 0x00064027.
second partiton has boot flag [COLOR="SeaGreen"]80[/COLOR].
"55 aa" is the msdos signature.
```

pxw@mbp:~$ sudo hexdump -Cn66 -s446 /dev/sda
000001be  00 fe ff ff ee fe ff ff  [COLOR="Red"]01 00 00 00[/COLOR] 27 40 06 00  |............'@..|
000001ce  [COLOR="SeaGreen"]80[/COLOR] fe ff ff af fe ff ff  28 40 06 00 48 c5 e1 04  |........(@..H...|
000001de  00 fe ff ff af fe ff ff  70 05 ec 04 00 00 40 01  |........p.....@.|
000001ee  00 fe ff ff 83 fe ff ff  70 05 2c 06 c0 df 00 05  |........p.,.....|
000001fe  55 aa                                             |U.|
00000200

```

There is a gptsync package for linux.
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1117464](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1117464)
 ----------------

Any one can register and contribute to the community wiki or create a new one.
This is another good intro page for the documentation for intel mac models.
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/MactelSupportTeam/CommunityHelpPages](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/MactelSupportTeam/CommunityHelpPages).

### Post by decoder_oh on 2009-04-28
> **pxwpxw said:**
> OK but still need raid modules, so what raid modules is linux loading?


You seem to misunderstand me. The card has it's own PCI device and vendor ID.

_NO_ driver in the linux kernel will load for this device without modifications. The problem is to find out if any of the drivers are compatible anyway, which can only be tested by adding the device/vendor ID pair to the respective driver. Unfortunately I did not succeed with the LSI drivers so far :)

### Post by decoder_oh on 2009-04-29
> **bean123 said:**
> It's possible that apple rebrand raid controller from other vendor under its name, but the device id 008a can't be found anywhere, so there is no way to tell. Perhaps you need to stick with external card for now.


Yes, this is what I think too. There is another problem with this card: It isn't assigned a valid IRQ. When I check via sysfs what IRQ this card has, it says 0. I wanted to try the megaraid drivers with this card and they also complained about not being able to allocate IRQ 0 for the card. As far as I know, IRQ handling is done via ACPI in this machine, but I don't know enough about it to be able to fix this.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-05-03
> **imkerberos said:**
> I have not use grub.cfg. all boot option use command line.
I am not using fakebios.

```

    linux (hd0,5)/debian/vmlinuz 
    initrd (hd0,5)/debian.initrd.gz
    boot

```
Boot debian i386 netinst image. VESA fb work ok, but not keyboard in "Select Language" screen.

```

    linux (hd0,5)/debian/vmlinuz acpi=force
    initrd (hd0,5)/debian/initrd.gz
    boot

```
The same as above, although have not keyboard.

```

    linux (hd0,5)/ubuntu/vmlinuz noefi video=efifb
    initrd (hd0,5)/ubuntu/initrd.gz
    boot

```
Boot ubuntu 9.04 AMD 64bit kernel. efifb is OK, but not keyboard on initramfs shell.

```

    linux (hd0,5)/ubuntu/vmlinuz noefi video=efifb acpi=force
    initrd (hd0,5)/ubuntu/initrd.gz
    boot

```
The same as above, not keyboard on initramfs shell.

Try using fakebios as first command in all of them
And linux commandline  needs root=/dev/sdxx
(except for the installers)

Example -

Boot ubuntu 9.04 AMD 64bit kernel on sda5
```

fakebios   
linux (hd0,5)/ubuntu/vmlinuz root=/dev/sda5 noefi video=efifb
initrd (hd0,5)/ubuntu/initrd.gz
boot

```

### Post by vasi on 2009-05-06
Hey, I've been trying to get Linux booting from USB on my MacBook4,1 (Intel X3100, 4GB RAM). I've had a few failures, but thanks to all your work on this I've finally mostly succeeded.

What worked:
[LIST]
[*] Ubuntu Jaunty amd64 Desktop, with e820.c patched according to post #280
[*] grub-efi 64-bit r2194
[*] The following load commands:
```

set root=(hd0,2)
fix_video
loadbios /boot/vbios.bin /boot/int10.bin
linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.28-11-generic-4g root=/dev/sdb2 video=efifb
initrd /initrd.img

```
[/LIST]

What didn't work:
[LIST]
[*] Ubuntu Intrepid i386 (32-bit), it got stuck in that ata4 error loop
[*] Kernels without the e820.c patch, they would freeze immediately after 'boot'.
[*] Using 'noefi' to fix the freezing. It would get a bit farther, but not much. Screenshot: [http://www.flickr.com/photos/90911042@N00/3507672588/sizes/l/](http://www.flickr.com/photos/90911042@N00/3507672588/sizes/l/)
[*] I don't have a working console unless I add agp=off, fix_video doesn't seem to be helping.
[/LIST]

I'd appreciate any advice re: the console. Also, is there a list of working configurations somewhere? I'd like to add what I ended up with, so the next person doesn't have to spend three days of trial and error :-)

Thanks so much to everyone who helped get EFI boot working!

---

### Post by vasi on 2009-05-06
> **nTia89 said:**
> 
now what i must do ????

i can't find anywhere the file "grub.efi"

You have to create the grub image:
```

./grub-mkimage -p /efi/grub -d . -o grub.efi apple appleldr cat chain configfile cpio date ext2 echo fat gpt help hexdump hfs hfsplus iso9660 linux ls normal pc reboot reiserfs scsi search sleep xfs boot minicmd sh

```

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-05-06
> **vasi said:**
> [In this post]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6808818&postcount=280"), where it mentions making the kernel map the whole 4G area. Look for the part of the post containing ' e820_end_of_ram_pfn'.



Yes, neither of them seemed to help. I also tried 'noefi', as well as 'pci=biosirq', neither helped.


Any ideas about why I still don't have a console with agp=off, even though I have fix_video? Also, do you know why 'noefi' doesn't boot, and I have to patch the kernel instead?

That is the first report of using the bean123 post #280 kernel e820.c patch, I have not used it . All reports so far have been using unpatched kernel and just loadbios with the noefi option for ubuntu amd64 with agp, or just fakebios and noefi for fbdev with amd64.

 
I am using (SVN rev 2130) noefi for MBPro4,1 with 4GB ram and imac8,1 with 4GB,
with efifb and xorg driver fbdev or radeon. 

The MacBook4,1 with intel X3100 and 4GB ram is also a new case.
There is another jaunty MB4,1 thread dealing with intel video issues for straight grub-pc-bios booting, may be relevant.

If you run 
  grub> fix_video 
 from command line, it will return video chip recognition, it was set up for intel 945/965, possibly ineffective for your case.

If fix_video and the agp=off option do not work, the last resort to enable console video is a blacklist. However this also depends on the Mac model and video chip - intel or otherwise. It is not necessary for MBP41 and Imac81 for example when using efifb and xorg fbdev or radeon driver.

Code for fix_video, fakebios, loadbios is in grub2/commands/efi fixvideo.c, loadbios.c.


So I cant quickly answer all points, that's all I can think of ATM, I am sure there is more to be considered.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-05-07
> **nTia89 said:**
> well !!!!

i've  done this 
and after this from Mac OS X
```
sudo bless --folder=/GRUB --file=/GRUB/efi/grub/grub.efi --setBoot

```
i've restart and i have grub like boot manager 

but i can't select any SO ....

i'm in a shell like Terminal but i don't know what i must do to boot Mac OS X

list the grub hd partitions  from the grub command line using 
```

sh:grub> ls
sh:grub> search /vmlinuz

```

grub.efi needs a grub.cfg menu  put beside it in /GRUB/efi/grub/

Try this one for a start, but you will need to change the hd and /dev/sd numbers for your installation.

```

#  grub.cfg pxw 20090508
timeout=20
default=1
fallback=1
set F1=ctrl-x
set color_normal=yellow/red

menuentry "OSX" {
	search --set /usr/standalone/i386/boot.efi
	chainloader /usr/standalone/i386/boot.efi
}
menuentry "ub810-x86 sdb2" {
 fakebios
 root=hd0,2
 linux  /vmlinuz root=/dev/sdb2  video=efifb
 initrd  /initrd.img
}
menuentry "u904-64 sda4 fbdev" {
 fakebios
 root=hd0,2
 linux  /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda4 video=efifb noefi
 initrd  /initrd.img
}
menuentry "CD" {
   appleloader CD
}
menuentry "MBR" {
   appleloader HD
}
menuentry "REBOOT" {
	reboot
}

```

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-05-09
> **vasi said:**
> 
I was previously insmod'ing fixvideo. I tried building it in, and that works!

Yes, don't know why insmod should fail, must be too late in startup I suppose.

> 
As soon as X starts, the console goes scrambled. This is a known bug, right?

Yes, and it varies, e.g. happens for most intel graphics, does not happen for radeon and nvidia on some Imac and MBP.

> 
It looks like loadbios isn't doing anything for me, I can take it out with no problem

Yes, I dont need anything except fix_video on MB2,1 intel for i386 kernel.
Sometimes just acpi=force for installer kernel.

But loadbios is needed on Imac with grub64.efi when using ATI fglrx, which only works for ubuntu810 2.6.27-x86, or debian 2.6.26-amd64. 

> 
Sometimes when I reboot, rEFIt does not show my USB key as an option. If I reboot a second time, that always works. Any idea why this occurs?

Nope, except there is some sort of hangover effect between Apple restarts that is sometimes good and sometimes bad, shutdown/restart get different results.

We could use a set of specific minimal working menuentry examples for the various Mac models, graphics, kernels etc, it is dificult to generalize.
Maybe annex to the Ubuntu wiki page for efi booting -

  [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/MactelSupportTeam/EFI-Boot-Mactel](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/MactelSupportTeam/EFI-Boot-Mactel)

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-05-09
Some interesting recent grub2 efi related patches.
[http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-05/](http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-05/)
Sat May 09 22:19:28 2009 

# [PATCH] trampoline for linux on 64-bit systems, Vladimir 'phcoder' Serbinenko, 05:40
[http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-05/msg00138.html](http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-05/msg00138.html)
Comment - Testing. relates to grub efi 64 bit startup issues. 
-------------------
gptsync committed rev 2201
[http://svn.savannah.gnu.org/viewvc?view=rev&root=grub&revision=2201](http://svn.savannah.gnu.org/viewvc?view=rev&root=grub&revision=2201)
# Re: [PATCH] gptsync, Vladimir 'phcoder' Serbinenko, 07:05
[http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-05/msg00140.html](http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-05/msg00140.html)
Comment - this is grub-dev strict interpretation of rules for hybrid gpt/mbr disks.
Existing Apple gpt and refit and linux gptsync treat the EFI partiton #1 differently in relation to gpt protective. They may conflict. Try it on a usb stick. gptsync.mod.

Apple Technical Note TN2166
Secrets of the GPT
[http://developer.apple.com/technotes/tn2006/tn2166.html](http://developer.apple.com/technotes/tn2006/tn2166.html)

-------------------
#[PATCH] bless command, Vladimir Serbinenko, 2009/04/18
[http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-04/msg00432.html](http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-04/msg00432.html)
Comment - Testing - works fine for simpler equivalent to OSX bless --folder --file, but not --setBoot yet.
 And grub.efi can bless itself, just needs to be loaded first - e.g. by rEFIt CD (no OSX).
You can build hfspbless.mod using the patch.
-------------------

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-05-10
> **vasi said:**
> I added my machine as a tested configuration on the wiki page: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/MactelSupportTeam/EFI-Boot-Mactel#MacBook4,1](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/MactelSupportTeam/EFI-Boot-Mactel#MacBook4,1)

Everyone else, feel free to go ahead and add yours too!

Thanks, good format, I will do likewise.

Just to clarify the unusable console effect - does this happen on th MB41 with fix_video when using the xorg.conf  Device=fbdev? 
If there is no problem problem when using fbdev, I just have a simple /etc/init.d/swxconfig script to switch between alternative xorg.conf for agp or fbdev - this one for Imac81 with xorg driver  ati fglrx or fbdev.

```

#! /bin/sh
# /etc/init.d/swxconfig

  cd /etc/X11
  grep efifb /proc/cmdline >/dev/null && cp xorg.conf-fbdev xorg.conf ||
  cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf-fglrx /etc/X11/xorg.conf

```

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-05-10
> **pxwpxw said:**
> Thanks, good format, I will do likewise.

Just to clarify the unusable console effect - does this happen on th MB41 with fix_video when using the xorg.conf  Device=fbdev? 
If there is no problem problem when using fbdev, I just have a simple /etc/init.d/swxconfig script to switch between alternative xorg.conf for agp or fbdev - this one for Imac81 with xorg driver  ati fglrx or fbdev.

```

#! /bin/sh
# /etc/init.d/swxconfig

  cd /etc/X11
  grep efifb /proc/cmdline >/dev/null && cp xorg.conf-fbdev xorg.conf ||
  cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf-fglrx /etc/X11/xorg.conf

```

The unusable console effect is not related to x server, it's caused by agpgart, so agp=off is the only alternative solution besides fix_video.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-05-10
> **bean123 said:**
> The unusable console effect is not related to x server, it's caused by agpgart, so agp=off is the only alternative solution besides fix_video.

That fits the MacBook2,1 with intel 945.

But then is it the same for the MB4,1 with intel 965? I am not sure, I think you have one or is it a MBPro?

Then again the group like the MBPro4,1 with nvidia and the IMAC8,1 with radeon, where the xorg driver does affect the console result, and the agpgart module is loaded all the time, and fix_video has no effect.

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-05-11
> **pxwpxw said:**
> That fits the MacBook2,1 with intel 945.

But then is it the same for the MB4,1 with intel 965? I am not sure, I think you have one or is it a MBPro?

Then again the group like the MBPro4,1 with nvidia and the IMAC8,1 with radeon, where the xorg driver does affect the console result, and the agpgart module is loaded all the time, and fix_video has no effect.

I don't know what happens with the new Imac with nvidia.

yeah, I have a MB4,1. The problem with intel driver is that it scratch the agp area in console mode (needs_scratch_page = true), and efi map the framebuffer to a non-zero offset, which would be overwritten. fix_video remap framebuffer to zero offset to avoid this issue. nvidia and radeon driver doesn't touch agp in console mode, therefore no problem.

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-05-14
20090514
Seen on grub-devel -
grub2 and fat efi files on latest Apples, James Jarvis, 11:36 May 13,2009
[http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-05/msg00161.html](http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-05/msg00161.html)

I tried it, it works for Imac8,1(64bit) MacBook2,1(32bit).

Attached gzippped  grub2202fat.efi runs in 32bit or 64bit efi macs.
(tested MB2,1 and IMAC8,1)

It was done in OSX10.5.6 using fatglue.py from refit source.
```

 ./fatglue.py grub2202fat.efi grub2202-32.efi grub2202-64.efi

```
(Size is grub32 + grub64).

preloaded modules -
```
 
appleldr boot cat cmp chain configfile crc date echo ext2 fixvideo fat fs_uuid gpt gptsync halt help hexdump hfs  hfsplus iso9660 linux loopback loadbios lspci ls minicmd memrw ntfs pc pci reboot reiserfs read scsi sleep search sh video videotest xfs

```

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-06-17
Ubuntu karmic has < grub-efi_1.96+20090523-1ubuntu1_amd64.deb >, which is up to SVN grub2 rev 2233 - with all the Apple Intel Mac revs from grub devs Bean and phcoder, you can install this in ubuntu and then run grub-mkimage from linux to build your grub.efi with preloaded modules. It is not necessary or useful to run grub-install for Apple, you just copy grub.efi to a hfsplus partition as usual. The package installation puts all  grub modules in default /usr/...
```

 $ grub-mkimage  -o grubkarmic.efi `cat modules`

```

grub-dumpbios is there if needed for loadbios.

To do  this from jaunty, requires the karmic grub-efi and the grub-common .debs and use dpkg -i *.deb - all the other depends seem to be present in jaunty (amd64) when I did it.


I used the 64 bit version with preloaded modules list as above post #762, all ok as expected for the svn rev 2233.

The karmic grub-pc package grub2 pc-bios build is the same vintage so should be good, but conflicts with the efi package if a default install.

(The jaunty grub2 package old version is no good for grub-efi, but works for grub-pc).

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-06-23
For external booting -

Attached tarball for grub64.efi binary from the grub2 grub-efi package in my post #766 above, contains -

```

efi folder -
  1474 22 Apr 18:15 grub-dumpbios
   204 23 Jun 14:50 grubefi-karmic
efi/grubefi-karmic folder
    1706 23 Jun 14:40 grub.cfg
  514048 16 Jun 19:05 grub64.efi
   26268 20 Oct  2008 grub64.icns

```
extract in Mac OSX Archive Utility or using 
```

$ tar -zxvf grub-efi64.tar.gz

```

Move the efi folder to the root of an hfsplus (MacOSX extended) partition, or use existing reEFIt /efi folder and use rEFIt to start grub64.efi, and show the grub64.efi boot menu.
The version of grub.cfg is for use with an external drive, and will probably need editing to suit your installation.
See further info in comments at tail of the grub.cfg.

Note- this is for 64bit efi macs, some early models,  MacBook2,1 and earlier, need 32bit version for their 32bit efi.

These are the preloaded modules, should not require more atg this time.
 
```

appleldr boot cat cmp chain configfile crc date echo ext2 fixvideo fat fs_uuid gpt gptsync halt help hexdump hfs  hfsplus iso9660 linux loopback loadbios lspci ls minicmd memrw ntfs pc pci reboot reiserfs read scsi sleep search sh video videotest xfs

```

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-06-23
> **TBD said:**
> thank you, but it didn't work :/

Very similar to result on MBP41 for ubuntu810 booting nvidia drivers,  can login blind to what seems like desktop, but too dim to see, however Xorg.0.log shows that xorg ran the log. 

It means you need to use xorg driver fbdev to get a desktop and see what more can be done.

I dont remember what you are using there, but try this -

--------------
menuentry "Linux" {
       search --set /vmlinuz
       fakebios
       fix_video
       linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda3 video=efifb noefi single
       initrd /initrd.img
}
-----------
(leave out splash)

Then from the root console -
check /etc/X11/xorg.conf and /var/log/Xorg.0.log
to see what xorg driver (or default) is loaded, if any.

Then -
Save xorg.conf to xorg.conf-0
Then create xorg.conf-fbdev, just this one section will do -
-----------------
Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
	Driver		"fbdev"
EndSection
-------------------
Nothing more

copy that to /etc/X11/xorg.conf

Then continue on from root console to try that one for a desktop.

```

by looking at the xorg.conf in the terminal i even discovered why, when i disactivated the dontzap option, xorg crashed (i thought of reporting a bug about it, but never bothered^^): i copypasted via gedit from some site the 3 lines... and the """ became some other characters :o

however this wasn't the issue (the dontzap line was commented out), the problem was that i activated the accelmethod uxa (due to the intel driver "regressions" in jaunty, it works far better than the default... which is exa i guess...)

i didn't think about it (besides without virtual terminal i was unable to try changing it ) since i used it for 2 days without any problem (but without ever rebooting... i make heavy use of standby)...

and i'm still using it: i uncommented the line... close the gnome session (hence Xorg) and gdm worked as normal....

i don't get why only when booting gdm gets disrupted by uxa, while otherwise works flawlessy...

however i think that issue is now OT, so i should ask on some other topic...

thank you again^^

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-06-23
Yes there are some other recent issues with intel video, other threads, but with intel you should have 3d accel I expect. OK if its fixed, thats good.
That empty xorg.conf is normal, its just gets the default intel driver as shown in the Xorg.0.log.

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-06-29
[COLOR="Red"]Grub2 Ubuntu Wiki[/COLOR]

This a new wiki (last month).
Good intro to the Grub2 packages current status and testing, common features for grub-pc grub-efi, needs a separate treatment for grub.efi on apple (the default assumption is that all computers are pc's).

[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Grub2](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Grub2)
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/Grub2Testing](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/Grub2Testing)

The Grub2 source can be configured for the packages grub-efi grub-pc grub-ieee1275(ppc open firmware), the grub-common package is used for all.

---

### Post by jlmcp on 2009-06-30
Success ... at least initial success. I will update here if further success is achieved.

I downloaded the attached grub2-x64 compile posted above and was able to boot up to the GRUB menu using my brand new MacBookPro5,5. (Mid year 2009 models that were just released after the recent WWDC). In addition to doing this from a USB thumb drive, I was also able to load up GRUB, et al onto a SD card and make use of the new SD slot on the latest models. 

Something's starting to work ...

Jake

> **pxwpxw said:**
> For external booting -

Attached tarball for grub64.efi binary from the grub2 grub-efi package in my post #766 above, contains ... etc.

All I did and how far I come has been written below.

Xserve1,1
Dual-Core Intel Xeon 
Processor Speed: 2 Ghz
Number of processors: 2
Total number of cores: 4
L2 Cache (per Processor): 4 MB
Memory: 8 GB
Bus Speed: 1.33 GHz
Boot Rom version: XS11.0080.B01

LOM Revision: 1.2.7

OSX 10.5.7 server
Developertools: 3.1.2
refit: 0.13

Want to install:
Centos 5.3 for hosting (cpanel)
(if centos is working I won't use OSX)

What I have so far:
used grub32.efi from post [http://www.backports.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6966036&postcount=523](http://www.backports.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6966036&postcount=523)

Created a folder named: install on my boot HD
with the files: initrd.img and vmlinuz in it. (taken from centos install disk)

(didn't install grub 2 myself due to installation errors, even after the suggested patches)

== Efi folder content:
grub-dumpbios
/grubefi-karmic
/grubefi-karmic/grub.cfg
/grubefi-karmic/grub32.efi
/grubefi-karmic/grub64.icns
/grubefi-karmic/initrd.gz
/grubefi-karmic/initrd.img (taken from centos install disk)
/grubefi-karmic/linux
/grubefi-karmic/pxelinux.0
/grubefi-karmic/vmlinuz (taken from centos install disk)
/refit
/tools

Grub config:
# grub.cfg pxw 20090316

timeout=10
default=0

set F1=ctrl-x
set F2=ctrl-c

menuentry "0-testfakebios" {
# for debugging set debug=efi
	hexdump -s 0xc0000 (mem)
	fakebios
	hexdump -s 0xc0000 (mem)
# deliberate error to get wait for key
	xxx
}
# 2.6.29 kernel
menuentry "1-debian sid x86_64 fbdev disk1" {
	fakebios
	root=disk1
	linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/hd1 video=efifb noefi
	initrd /initrd.img
}
# 2.6.28 CD installer
menuentry "2-ubuntu x86_64 alternate installer cd" {
	fakebios
	search --set /install/vmlinuz
	linux /install/vmlinuz video=efiifb noefi
	initrd /install/initrd.img
}
# 2.6.26 net installer
menuentry "3-debian lenny64 net install images" {
	fakebios
	search --set /lenny64/linux
	linux /lenny64/linux video=efiifb
	initrd /lenny64/initrd.gz
}
menuentry "REBOOT" {
	reboot
}

Thanks in advance for any help.

OK, menuentry has bugs

Add this one to chack bootability, should load an installer or similar (which does not require linux  root=/dev/xxx) then you can refine it.
```

menuentry "Test" {
	fakebios
	search --set /vmlinuz
	linux /vmlinuz video=efifb
	initrd /initrd.gz
}

```

Use the sh:grub> command line in grub.efi to find all your partitions
and all your /vmlinuz 

```

> ls -l
> search /vmlinuz
> set pager=1
> ls -l (hd0,1)/
> help

```

IDK anything about Slax or its kernel comandline options, but you must have video=efifb and add noefi if its 64bit kernel.

Try that.

EDIT-
Having tried Slax and fedora, video=efifb is not required for them, command line is different, fakebios has noeffect, but fix_video needed on intel MacBook2,1.

Clarification - I got it wrong.

Heres the story as I see it -

You don't need grub.efi to do the installation, and it has not been doing anything.

Just use the installer CD and install CentOS, booting from the CD, leaving MacOSX and grub.efi all working, you need both after the install.

Only then will you need grub.efi to restart the installed centos,
and you will need MacOSX for internet and if you need help with grub.efi.

I checked the i386 Centos network install cd (small ~ 10MB) on MacBook2,1 and it all worked from CD - no keyboard problem. (The x86_64 Centos is useless on IMAC81 )

I can't help you further with the centos installation, there are some options in the help menu at the start that may help with the keyboard, but it has nothing to do with grub.efi.

Never mind about the debian test for now.

When you get Centos installed (or any linux ), we can look at any problems with booting it using grub.efi.

### Post by jlmcp on 2009-07-01
@pxwpxw

OK! More success. Played with the GRUB/cmdline 'search' and 'ls' tools to determine that actual root of my thumb drive. Turns out its hd0,2 as someone suggested above. Apparently Mac's do index removable media before the internal HDD, so hd0 is the flash drive and hd1 is the internal HDD ... in my setup that is. I suspect that if I had additional flash drives inserted at boot they would be indexed before the HDD as well ... but I digress.

Turns out, by moving the boot flags from the initrd line to the linux in the menuentry, I can boot Slax 6 from GRUB2/EFI. 

```
menuentry "Slax Graphics Mode - Test 1" {
	fakebios
	root=hd0,2
	linux /boot/vmlinuz ramdisk_size=6666 root=/dev/ram0 rw autoexec=xconf changes=/slax/
	initrd /boot/initrd.gz
}
```

I also had to remove the 'telinit~4' flag, which is supposed to auto-login with a root shell on boot.  GRUB complained that it had no idea what to do with that flag, but then it was copied over from the native Slax menuentry under LILO. Maybe there is a GRUB equivalent? Is that the 'single' flag?

Also, after logging in, I tried to run the usual 'xconf' and 'startx' but no go.  Linux complains of "no screen". I think I read in the thread here that there were some necessary video modifiers for EFI boots ... ? Am I right on that? When I boot the same image off the CD-Rom it works just fine. Pretty sure this is 32-bit kernel.

Manual xorg.conf configuration? 

> **pxwpxw said:**
> 

...

IDK anything about Slax or its kernel comandline options, but you must have video=efifb and add noefi if its 64bit kernel.

Try that.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-01
**@gfive**

I located the fix for ROM image present post #639, may well be the bug with fakebios failing (no kbd, mouse), I cant find it in rev 2202, I will check current svn trunk and do the fix tomorrow for 32bit.
However I am not sure until I get you to try it.

There was also a problem with 4GB RAM or more, also believe fixed.
Do you have 4GB or more?

This is copy of the fakebios fix for my freference

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=7082840&postcount=639](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=7082840&postcount=639)

```

commands/efi/loadbios.c:

static int
enable_rom_area (void)
{
  grub_pci_address_t addr;
  grub_uint32_t *rom_ptr;

  rom_ptr = (grub_uint32_t *) VBIOS_ADDR;
  if ((*rom_ptr != BLANK_MEM) && (*rom_ptr != 0)) // change this line
    {
      grub_printf ("ROM image present.\n");
      return 0;
    }
...

```
============

### Post by vasi on 2009-07-01
> **pxwpxw said:**
> add noefi if its 64bit kernel.


On my MacBook4,1, the Karmic kernel doesn't need noefi! (Which is great, because noefi wasn't always working.) Do you know why this is? Has Ubuntu included a new patch? Or is this just something about 2.6.30?

Regarding hd0 vs hd1: In some cases, the order that the disks load can be random. It's best to use 'search' with a UUID to automatically set the root.

Finally, the problem I was having with the keyboard in Debian-installer is apparently because of a problem with the init scripts. If I replace the kernel (Debian initrd + Karmic kernel + Karmic initrd kernel modules) it still doesn't work. But if I replace the initrd (Karmic initrd + Debian kernel + modules) then it does work.

---

### Post by jlmcp on 2009-07-02
@pxwpxw

> **pxwpxw said:**
> 
Please see refs at post #1 for basic grub.efi info.
Also the "search this thread" works quite well. 

Did some reading, and see where you posted comments about using an fbdev xorg.conf for graphics compatibility. It worked. In fact, as it turns out, Slax already had a 'xorg.conf-fbdev' config file in /etc/X11 so all I had to do was remove the existing .conf and replace it with .conf-fbdev version to get it working. For the record, I CAN boot Linux from both USB/SD-card media on an Apple MacBookPro5,5. Add that one to the list.

> **pxwpxw said:**
> If you install ubuntu or debian I can be more help, you lost me with slax.

Yeah, Slax is not Ubuntu and technically we are having this conversation on an Ubuntu forum. But a win for any flavor of Linux with EFI and GRUB2 is just that. They all have their eccentricities and purposes. Slax was designed/optimized to be a flavor of Linux that could be bootable from external media. A lot of devs use it a a basis for disk utilities and forensic tools in situations where you don't want to write to the HDD. 

Anyway, thanks for the help.

Jake

---

Finally, the problem I was having with the keyboard in Debian-installer is apparently because of a problem with the init scripts. If I replace the kernel (Debian initrd + Karmic kernel + Karmic initrd kernel modules) it still doesn't work. But if I replace the initrd (Karmic initrd + Debian kernel + modules) then it does work.

### Post by vasi on 2009-07-02
> **pxwpxw said:**
> Maybe the 5.3 centos kernel is not well configured for EFI.

CentOS has a pretty old kernel (2.6.18 ), and it's configured very conservatively, so I would not be surprised if it was missing EFI support. There are a few solutions I can think of:

[LIST=1]
[*] You could try to install CentOS, but using another distro's kernel. So you take the vmlinuz from another distro, and then you have to create an initrd. This can be complicated, basically you have to unpack (with cpio) the initrds from both CentOS and the other distro. Then you take the kernel modules from the other distro, and the rest of the initrd from CentOS, and repack them into a new initrd.

When I had trouble getting the keyboard working with the Debian installer, I installed Debian using the Karmic kernel this way. I can give more details if you want to give it a try.


[*] You could move the hard drive to another computer, install CentOS, recompile the kernel with whatever options you need for EFI support, and then put the drive back in the Xserve!


[*] A shortcut to the remove-the-hard-drive method is to use a virtual machine. You can arrange that VMWare Fusion is able to see your physical partitions--I think other VMs support this as well. Then you can use the VM to install CentOS and recompile the kernel.
[/LIST]

---

### Post by vasi on 2009-07-02
> **jlmcp said:**
> 
Slax was designed/optimized to be a flavor of Linux that could be bootable from external media.


A very cool feature of Slax is USB-from-CD mode. If you have a USB install of Slax, but it will not boot on a particular machine, you can just boot from the USB-from-CD disc. It will start the kernel, detect that the USB disk/stick is present, and then automatically start using the root filesystem from USB. This is very helpful on old PCs that don't support USB boot, or on Macs if you can't get grub-efi to work well.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-03
> **'[LocK] said:**
> I don't know that, that is right topic to discuss that, but is there any way or possibility to change already installed in mbr grub to efi grub ?

I try to install it using bless but after reboot it always boot using grub in mbr.

Any ideas ?

You install grub.efi on a hfsplus partition, grub on the mbr does not conflict at all, and can be used as an alternate boot.

You can use the full OSX bless command for an hfsplus partition to boot grub.efi directly.

If you want to install grub.efi pleas read the basic reference info linked on post #1, and the Mac OSX manual for bless (man bless).

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-03
> **vasi said:**
> Do you have rEFIt installed? I'm not sure if it's possible to do a grub-efi boot without it.
```

bless --folder / --file /grub.efi 

```

will boot grub.efi without refit, and unbless anything else on /.
But the refit blesser will reset it to refit after restart to OSX.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-07
> **pxwpxw said:**
> ( post #724 pushken 29 april 2009 ).


Seems to confirm a custom kernel config was sufficient at that date, pushken was using raid.

Checked the configuration for the current CentOS5.3 x86_64 kernel, and found that it is not configured for EFI.

The CentOS grub.efi problem is a general Apple Intel/grub.efi issue which may arise for distros other than ubuntu or debian  - i.e the debugging done on this thread was mainly with debian based kernel flavours so centos/ red hat/fedora based and others may differ significantly, and it is difficult to compare kernel versions.

The first requirement is for the following EFI config setings. These options may not be available on kernel source versions prior to 2.6.26 (unless modified).
```

pxw@im:~$ grep EFI /boot/config-2.6.26-2-amd64 
CONFIG_EFI=y
CONFIG_FB_EFI=y
CONFIG_EFI_VARS=m
CONFIG_EFI_PARTITION=y

pxw@im:~$ uname -a
Linux im 2.6.26-2-amd64 #1 SMP Fri Mar 27 04:02:59 UTC 2009 x86_64 GNU/Linux

```
The CentOS5.3 EFI config is -
```

[pxw@localhost ~]$ grep EFI /boot/config-2.6.18-128.el5 
# CONFIG_EFI is not set
CONFIG_EFI_PARTITION=y

```

All grub.efi information is of interest here, not just for ubuntu, it helps with development and application of grub.efi for all linux.

---

Note this machine has both the onboard Nvidia 9400M and the 9600M GT (when doing legacy boot, only the 9600M GT is available - hence why I'm trying to get efi boot working to enable the 9400M) - and so lspci only shows the 9600M GT:

Oh, you can also get pci information using rEFIt shell, it should be more accurate, enter the shell, use this command:

pci -b

It would dump pci devices, look for video card, for example, my mb shows this line:

00 00 02 00 ==> Display Controller - VGA/8514 controller

Then use this command to dump device information: (skip the first 00)

pci 00 02 00 -i

### Post by bean123 on 2009-07-12
> **arjanvm said:**
> read_word 0x40e
0x0
 read_word 0x413
0x0
 read_dword 0xc0000
0xffff
 read_dword 0xf0000
0x7fff

Btw, about rEFIt on this MBP 5,3 shows:

EFI ver 1.10
platform x86_64 (64 bit)
Firmware: Apple 1.10
Screen Output: Graphics Output (UEFI), 1440x900

Perhaps the new macs changes the method to enable rom. In this new grub64.efi, fakebios doesn't enable rom by default, to enable rom, add --enable-rom option:

fakebios --enable-rom

It's not critical to enable rom, but DMI information needs to be stored in 0xf0000 segment, so DMI detection may fail if --enable-rom is not used.

---

### Post by arjanvm on 2009-07-12
@bean123:
Not sure if this is of any help, but I have a working keyboard in the ash shell when I boot Slax with your latest grub64.efi, no kernel options.

root=(hd0,3)
fakebios

initrd /boot/initrd.img

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-07-12
> **arjanvm said:**
> @bean123:
Not sure if this is of any help, but I have a working keyboard in the ash shell when I boot Slax with your latest grub64.efi, no kernel options.

root=(hd0,3)
fakebios
linux /boot/vmlinuz
initrd /boot/initrd.img

If you boot x86_64 kernel and efi is usable, then fakebios is not needed, as kernel can get ACPI and DMI information from efi table.

But for some 2.6.27-2.6.68 kernel, there is a bug that cause it to hang, so you need to add noefi and therefore efi is not usable. The 2.6.26 series works well, or you can apply my patch to kernel (somewhere in this thread). I remember someone reports that the new 2.6.30 also works, although I haven't tried it myself.

If you boot i386 kernel, efi is not usable, even without noefi, as the bitness of kernel and firmware doesn't match. In this case, you can only rely on fakebios.

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-12
ubuntu 910
without fakebios

pxw@im:~$ uname -a
Linux im 2.6.30-8-generic #9-Ubuntu SMP Wed Jun 3 15:38:38 UTC 2009 x86_64 GNU/Linux
pxw@im:~$ cat /proc/cmdline 
BOOT_IMAGE=/vmlinuz root=/dev/sda8 video=efifb
pxw@im:~$ 
All go.
---------
Ubuntu 904 
with fakebios

pxw@im:~$ uname -a
Linux im 2.6.28-11-generic #42-Ubuntu SMP Fri Apr 17 01:58:03 UTC 2009 x86_64 GNU/Linux
pxw@im:~$ cat /proc/cmdline 
BOOT_IMAGE=/vmlinuz root=/dev/sda6 video=efifb noefi
pxw@im:~$ 
---------

### Post by pepe# on 2009-07-12
Thanks for the patch. It does not apply cleanly against svn, but this seem to be some whitespace issue only. However, after 

grub-mkimage -d . -o grub.efi hfsplus gpt fat ext2 normal chain boot configfile
cp grub.efi *.mod fs.lst command.lst /mnt/efi/grub-svn/

The menu is not working. It tells me that menuitem, appleloader, linux etc commands are all not available...Am I doing something wrong?


nvidia driver works with fakebios or loadbios, but the console is broken. Most notably, backlight brightness is also broken since mbp_nvidia_bl and also pommed's own nv8600gt driver both use some bios (SMI?) calls..

---

### Post by bean123 on 2009-07-12
> **pepe# said:**
> Thanks for the patch. It does not apply cleanly against svn, but this seem to be some whitespace issue only. However, after 

grub-mkimage -d . -o grub.efi hfsplus gpt fat ext2 normal chain boot configfile
cp grub.efi *.mod fs.lst command.lst /mnt/efi/grub-svn/

The menu is not working. It tells me that menuitem, appleloader, linux etc commands are all not available...Am I doing something wrong?


nvidia driver works with fakebios or loadbios, but the console is broken. Most notably, backlight brightness is also broken since mbp_nvidia_bl and also pommed's own nv8600gt driver both use some bios (SMI?) calls..

Sometimes grub.efi can't load external module, so you need to add them in grub-mkimage. You should at least add minicmd, sh, linux and appleldr.

### Post by alexmurray on 2009-07-15
> **pxwpxw said:**
> That is awesome indeed.

I have not yet been able to get 3d accel nvidia with grub.efi on MBP41 with Nvidia 8600, but have not tried with that latest by **bean123**. 
Was anything special, menuentry, driver, linux configuration, needed, if so could you please post for the record.
I just used grub64.efi from bean123 from [post 884]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=7598685&postcount=844"), with the grub.cfg etc from [post 772]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=7502407&postcount=772") and using the menu entry which has login with root as sda3 (which if I recall correctly has the options):

```
fakebios
```

and kernel command line options (noefi is needed for 64bit kernels)

```
video=efifb noefi
```

Then (for the MBP51 atleast) you just need to add the correct PCI device id to your xorg.conf so it uses the correct video card:

```

Section "Device"
    Driver      "nvidia"
    BusID       "PCI:03:00:0"
EndSection

```

And that should be it (note I am writing this from memory as am at work at the moment, will check later when I get home to make sure this is correct)

initrd /isolinux/initrd0.img
}

Installation went perfect.
Needed to use:

root@localhost]# nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

Section "Device"
Identifier "fbdev driver"
Driver "fbdev"
EndSection

[root@localhost]# exit


But Now I want to start my xserve with fedora.
The last code it shows when booting from the HD is:

sd 8:0:2:0: [sdc] write cache: enabled, read cache enables, doesn't supposrt DPO or FUD
	sdc: sdc1 sdc2
sd 8:0:2:0: [sdc] Attached SCSI disk
sd 8:0:2:0: Attached scsi Generic sg3 type 0

(I have 3 disks sda, sdb, sdc, all showing same info)
 
menuentry "Boot fedora 03" {
fakebios
root=hd0,3
linux /vmlinuz-2.6.29.4-167.fc11.x86_64 ro root=/dev/sda3 noefi acpi=force irqpoll rhgb nomodeset
initrd /initrd-2.6.29.4-167.fc11.x86_64.img
}

Any suggestions?

Hi,

The last seen startup code seems to be before console is enabled, so don't know if problem is loss of console video or some other hangup.

Grub root=hd0,3 seems to be correct because it is loading the kernel, 
but is linux root=/dev/sda3 correct? That could be confirmed by checking with the Live CD, or else try each one sda sdb sdc. I think you said you cant do Target Mode with the Xserve to access it from your MBP.

Also using a Live CD, you can edit the HD fedora /etc/X11/xorg.conf for Driver "fbdev". ( fedora live ok, debian live cd is good)

Also need to look at /etc/fstab to see what is written there to mount the root, should be be correct if it is a uuid.

If you need to add 'single' to stop before the desktop, it may need video=efifb to see the console text, I don't know if that works with fedora, but you used it for the live cd..

I have found fedora has problems with external firewire, but is ok with usb, however I assume your 3 drives are all internal.

Also  some Xserve had a problem with raid.

EDIT:

1. Fedora live cd can edit the HD xorg.conf, not a problem, debian live is good it has no installer, likewise slax.

2. The menuentry I use to boot fedora on an external drive has fakebios and fix_video for 32bit EFI (MB21), you may need that, but not using all the other options.
(boot partition ext3 sdb6, / ext4 sdb7)

```

menuentry "MB21 fedora-11-x86_64 167 sdb6/7" {
	fix_video
	fakebios
	root=hd0,6
	linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sdb7
	initrd /initrd.img
}
menuentry "IM81 fedora-11-x86_64 167 sdb6/7" {
	fakebios
	root=hd0,6
	linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sdb7
	initrd /initrd.img
}

```

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-21
> **gfive said:**
> Okay while installing is going well the following question.

a: should I use soft or hard partitions for the install. (/tmp /home etc)
b: if I use a soft partition, let's say on root=/dev/sda4 (how do I call the soft partition?)
c: How do I edit xorg.conf while I am in live CD?

I use fedora to install cpanel.net (it's for hosting websites etc)



Those questions are quite off topic for this thread and of a very general nature, you will get more results by posting new thread topics to get the help you need, not necessarily in the Apple sub forum. It is not appropriate to deal with them on this thread.

However, here are some comments that may help -

You may be trying to edit the wrong xorg,conf - the one on the Live CD - and you need to be root. I think fedora also uses "sudo su" to become root.

If you still have problems using the fedora live cd, try the debian live cd, it is also easier to boot. 

It needs fix_video to see the root console for 32bit MacBook21.
```

menuentry "debian501 x86_64 Live CD" {
	fix_video
        fakebios
        search --set -f /live/vmlinuz1
        linux /live/vmlinuz1 boot=live union=aufs single
        initrd /live/initrd1.img
}

```
Then at the root console just edit /etc/X11/xorg.conf for Driver "fbdev" and exit.
If you have it on a persistent Live USB stick it keeps the edit.

Come back here if you need more help with grub.efi, but please post other topics on another thread
.

### Post by alexmurray on 2009-07-23
As noted in the wiki, you'll need a version of grub2 which supports the graphics output protocol, but as far as I know this has not been merged into upstream yet (see [here]("http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-07/msg00152.html")). So you'll need to extract the tarball linked to from the wiki into the /efi directory (assuming you have installed rEFIt) on your os x partition, and then replace the grub64.efi with the latest one (also linked to from the wiki) that supports graphics output protocol.

---

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-24
> **Nikos.Alexandris said:**
> Hi! I have a question: I have already successfully installed grub2 (following directions at [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Grub2]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Grub2")). Now I would like to try-out the steps to get the MBP5,1 to work with the 9400 graphic card.

In the wiki ( [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/MacBookPro5-1_5-2/Jaunty]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/MacBookPro5-1_5-2/Jaunty") ) it is written to install grub2 in the efi directory. Hmm... ?
Do I need to make a clean install of grub2?
Do I need to remove the current installation/settings etc. (which, if I am right, are to be found under /boot/grub )?

Thanks for any help :-)
Nikos

I think you have the grub-pc version of grub2 which is the default.

Grub2 comes in package versions grub-pc or grub-efi as well as many other platforms. You want an efi bootloader for choice between the dual graphics on the MBP5x as noted in the MacBook5-2 wiki,
and **alexmurray** above.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-24
> **.ubik said:**
> I also have a MacBook Pro 5,3 and want to boot Ubuntu from a SD card, I'm happy to read arjanvm got it working :)
Is there any chance someone with skills could put together a more simple step by step manual on how to accomplish it? This thread is huge and rather confusing to me. I have never used grubs or rEFIt so that is all new.
Thanks in advance :)

This is my FIRST POST btw :D

Yes, its time to update some simple grub.efi installation doc after 89 pages, I will try to do so.

Meanwhile see the MBP5-2 page link and other basics for installing via the help.ubuntu.com cummunity help Mactel portal page -
 
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/MactelSupportTeam/CommunityHelpPages](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/MactelSupportTeam/CommunityHelpPages) 
Also see the stickies at the top of this apple sub forum.

Here is a starter for your usb card - 

The Ubuntu install procedure will be standard except to the usb card, and with  addition of a small partition for grub.efi, type hfsplus (Macos Extended but not jounalled)). 

1. Install rEFIt in your Mac OSX, get it working.

2. A suitable grub.efi is at post 849 as mentioned in the MBP52 page.

You can get started with grub.efi by formatting the usb card using Mac OSX Disk Utility. It needs to be about 4GB minimum, 8GB better.

Partition Option = GUID.

2 Partitions both Macosx Extended (Not journalled)

P1  size 100MB for grub.efi and extras. (grub.efi plus grub.cfg needs only about 1MB)

Partition 2 = the remainder, to be deleted and used by Ubuntu partitioner, or temprarily by OSX.

Then in Mac OSX download and copy grub.efi and grub.cfg to the P1.
Create directory in P1 named  'efi', open it and create directory named 'grub'.

Move grub.efi and grub.cfg into the grub directory.
So you have on the usb P1
/efi/grub/grub.efi, grub.cfg

rEFIt will detect grub.efi on the usb (if it is in /efi/grub/ on the usb, or if it is in /efi/grub on the OSX partition with  refit. You can then play with grub.efi and grub.cfg can be edited from OSX.

Then you have only to install Ubuntu in the Usb P2 space, install without grub bootloader because you will be using grub.efi and the installer would install grub-pc.

Finally restarting to Mac OSX you can edit the grub.cfg menu to boot Usb ubuntu, or you can bopt it manually from the grub.efi command line, but you will need to see some examples of menuentry for that, in the wiki pages. or in this thread.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-24
elilo.efi from ubuntu package elilo 3.8-1ubuntu1 is booting ubuntu910 on IMAC81, MBP41, and MBP5x,  on kernel 2.6.30-8-generic. MBP5x using Gop need an efi boot to enable choice between dual graphics hardware.

elilo 3.8-1ubuntu1 supports Graphic Output Protocol. (Gop).

Current (20090724 grub-efi and SVN grub2 ) do no yet have Gop support for EFI, patched in binary at post #849. patch at grub-devel Bean July 12 2009 ------ under disciussion, not yet committed.

[PATCH] Support graphics output protocol in EFI
 [http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-07/msg00152.html](http://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2009-07/msg00152.html)

I am  starting  a thread for elilo on Apple.

The MBP-2 wiki is not mine, you are free to fix it, it is just a brief mention of using grub.efi for a specific purpose, and any expansion should go to that wiki page, no point posting it here.

I will try to keep any grub.efi pages separate and not Mac model specific, as this thread is intended to be.

The  **[http://grub.enbug.org/TestingOnMacbook](http://grub.enbug.org/TestingOnMacbook)** is written by grub dev Bean and quite up to date and good reference, but not detailed or specific to MacBook, however it takes in most of the debugging by bean123 on this thread.

The case problem with EFI v/s efi was fixed, /efi/ is fine for hfsplus file system, also is converted by default to EFI on the Apple EFI partition (200MB fat32), I have not had a problem there lately with grub.efi.

You dont have to uninstall grub2-pc, there is no conflict with grub.efi when a prebuilt grub.efi such as 
post #849 is manually installed in a hfsplus partition. It is indeed useful to have both available to boot via altenate methods, as I do. 

However if you should try to install the ubuntu grub-efi package in Ubuntu, it will need to uninstall the grub-pc package because they conflict.

The procedure outlined in my post #884 should do to get started, except it was a quick answer to installing on an external drive, so for a HD install, you put grub.efi and grub.cfg in /efi/grub/ on your OSX partition with rEFIt in /efi/refit/.

You will have to do some editing to grub.cfg for your system, but any grub.cfg will let you get the grub.efi boot menu displayed and see how it works. 

The grub.cfg language for grub2 is common to grub-pc and grub-efi (as is the grub-common package). However there are also specific different commands and kernel commandline options.
(fakebios, loadbios, fix_video and video=efifb, noefi refer to the Testing on Macbook page)

### Post by Nikos.Alexandris on 2009-07-27
> **pxwpxw said:**
> Pleas post the grub.cfg menuentry you are using to boot with grub.efi, there may be something you can change there.

Here is my slightly customized grub.cfg:

```
# grub.cfg for external drive pxw 20090623

timeout=20
default=0


# various settings
set F1=ctrl-x
set F2=ctrl-c
set color_normal=yellow/blue


# settings taken from "native" (ubuntu) /boot/grub/grub.cfg
set root=(hd0,3)
search --fs-uuid --set f1a75acd-80e7-4488-abdb-a138aa6c5ff0


# menuentries
menuentry "OSX" {
	search --set /usr/standalone/i386/boot.efi
	chainloader /usr/standalone/i386/boot.efi
}
menuentry "internal OSX on sda2" {
	root=hd1,2
	chainloader /usr/standalone/i386/boot.efi
}


# custom menuentries
menuentry "Ubuntu, linux 2.6.28-14-generic" {
	fakebios
	linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.28-14-generic root=UUID=f1a75acd-80e7-4488-abdb-a138aa6c5ff0 ro video=efifb noefi quiet splash usbcore.autosuspend=1 hpet=force
	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.28-14-generic
}

menuentry "Ubuntu, linux 2.6.28-14-generic (single-user mode)" {
	linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.28-14-generic root=UUID=f1a75acd-80e7-4488-abdb-a138aa6c5ff0 ro video=efifb noefi single 
	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.28-14-generic
}


# with root=hd1,3
menuentry "internal sda3 fbdev root console" {
	fakebios
	root=hd1,3
	linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.28-14-generic root=/dev/sda3 video=efifb noefi single
	initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.28-14-generic
}
menuentry "internal sda3 fbdev login" {
	fakebios
	root=hd1,3
	linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.28-14-generic root=/dev/sda3 video=efifb noefi
	initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.28-14-generic
}


# internal cd
menuentry "internal CD" {
   appleloader CD
}

# internal MBR(1) == as booting from grub2 ?
menuentry "internal MBR1" {
   appleloader HD
}


# reboot
menuentry "REBOOT" {
	reboot
}
```


I can boot with both the **"Ubuntu, linux 2.6.28-14-generic"** and the "**internal sda3 fbdev login"**.

I guess this is explained in some documentation why hd1 and not hd0 (...the comments in the end of the grub.cfg are not that clear to me). I wonder if the **set root=(hd0,3)** I've put in the very beginning of the file is useless/not correct.

Thanks a lot!

If you have an external drive or usb stick, that will change grub drive numbers, and with one usb stick, first internal HD is usually hd1, stick is hd0.

Settings from /boot/grub.cfg are an example (from grub2-pc?), irrelevant here. You need root= setting in each menuentry to be safe.

Is it the amd64 kernel?

Re the icns, its just renamed from the refit files - there's a bunch of them.
If you want to create one, 'google apple icns' should point you in right direction, you need the icon utility from the OSX Xcode tools, developer.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-27
You can see how the grub drive name allocation is affected if you use the grub> command line
Some useful commands (which should also work for grub2-pc) -
```

grub> TAB
grub> set
grub> set pager=1
grub> help
grub> ls
grub> ls -l
grub> search -f /vmlinuz
grub> lspci
grub> lsmod

```
Any drive plugged in at restart affects result.

---

### Post by Nikos.Alexandris on 2009-07-27

Any drive plugged in at restart affects result.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-27
> **Nikos.Alexandris said:**
> That's not so good, is it? If I understand this correctly, depending on the type and the number of external drives plugged at power-on, grub-efi settings might be rendered useless?

Well not useless, but you may have to have a menuentry to account for each startup drive configuration if you change it. But you can plug in a usb stick or other device after startup without problem.

One solution is to use 'search' and the partition UUID in grub menuentry, (I think shown in the grub2 install example grub.cfg? ) and grub2 documentation. I have not tried it. But "ls -l" in grub.efi commandline lists hdx,x and UUID xxxxxx . You might like to investigate that and tell us the result.

Another method is to put a unique marker file on the root of the partition and use search to set root for it 
```

 search --set -f /foobar123

```
Of course if you only have one /vmlinuz in the system, search for that.

Again, with elilo.efi default install for HD boot, the problem does no arise, because the kernel is copied to the elilo EFI local partition, and kernel can then use linux device numbers (i.e. /dev/sda stays as /dev/sda ), or UUID.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-28
**@ Nikos.Alexandris**

You need more specific identification of any grub.efi associated problems for you Mac model, it may need grub-dev attention. Its not clear to me. I think you need to sort out on a special issues MBP5x thread first which issues are specifically grub.efi and whether we can solve in this forum. 

----------------------------------------------
I started an elilo.efi thread for elilo discussion,it is at a different stage and scope compared with  grub.efi, I am using it for Karmic.
[COLOR="Red"]elilo efi bootloader - apple macs[/COLOR]
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1221709](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1221709)

For elilo.efi and grub.efi,  on MBP41 I can only use fbdev, not nvidia driver, but that is also for Uga Draw graphics, whereas MBP5x is Gop which elilo supports , so it would be useful for someone with MBP5x to confirm what is the situation for 3d accel with elilo.

------------------------------------------------

I use a simple  script to switch automatically between xorg.conf for fbdev and other driver.

Made as follows -

All as root
```

1. create and make executable
/etc/init.d/swxconfig

#! /bin/sh
 cd /etc/X11
 grep fbdev /proc/cmdline >/dev/null && cp xorg.conf-fbdev xorg.conf ||
 cp xorg.conf-0 xorg.conf

2. Make symbolic link in /etc/rc2.d

cd /etc/rc2.d

ln -s ../init.d/swxconfig S20swxconfig

giving /etc/rc2.d/S20swxconfig -> ../init.d/swxconfig
(symbolic link to ../init.d/swxconfig)

3. Two files in /etc/X11, my xorg.conf-0 is the default one for no fbdev.

/etc/X11/xorg-0

/etc/x11/xorg-fbdev
==============
Then if you want fbdev, addd fbdev to the grub menuentry linux command line
i.e.
 
linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sdax... other cmdline options.. fbdev


```

### Post by alexmurray on 2009-07-30
> **Nikos.Alexandris said:**
> Alex,

any success on this one? How do you (really) use the proper *xorg.conf* depending on whether you boot on EFI or BIOS-mode? Currently I have a copy of *xorg.conf* for **nvidia9600** and one for **nvidia9400** and copy them over accordingly using the terminal whenever I "switch" from one boot-mode to the other.

Is there a way to have both in the xorg.conf? Or, is there a way to use the "correct" xorg.conf depending the boot-mode? I am confused... there is only one xorg.conf after all.

Regards, Nikos

Nikos - I just edit my xorg.conf to specify a different PCI device id (2 vs 3) when changing, so its basically the same.

Also, the reason nvidia_bl and applesmc are not working is because using fakebios (I think) means that we can't read the dmi product info from the bios (since we faked it) and so the machine is not correctly identified as a MacBookPro5,1

So hopefully running a later kernel (such as 2.6.31 in karmic), where we don't need noefi and hence no fakebios will allow it to id the machine correctly, but I havent yet tested this.

---

### Post by Nikos.Alexandris on 2009-07-30
> **alexmurray said:**
> Nikos - I just edit my xorg.conf to specify a different PCI device id (2 vs 3) when changing, so its basically the same.

Also, the reason nvidia_bl and applesmc are not working is because using fakebios (I think) means that we can't read the dmi product info from the bios (since we faked it) and so the machine is not correctly identified as a MacBookPro5,1

So hopefully running a later kernel (such as 2.6.31 in karmic), where we don't need noefi and hence no fakebios will allow it to id the machine correctly, but I havent yet tested this.

OK I got it (more or less). Thanks for the explanations.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-30
> **dsbrooks said:**
> suggestions for xorg.conf contents? haven't been able to get X11 running yet.

Thanks for confirming grub.efi working on Xseve3,1 and 
Screen Output: Graphics Output (UEFI), 1920x1200

Once you get to root console then - 

Procedure as used here, fedora live CD is quite slow to get to desktop:

1. At the Live CD root console -
```


[root@localhost]# 
[root@localhost]# nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

Section "Device"
 Identifier "fbdev driver"
 Driver "fbdev"
EndSection

[root@localhost]# ls -l /etc/X11

[root@localhost]# exit

```
( list /etc/X11 to check xorg,conf before exit. Then
exit or control d will then carry on with the startup to desktop )

2. After a few minutes this gets to a working Desktop with all apps and Install Option. Network should work if a wired network is connected (eth0).

**EDIT: for some graphics chips/mac models, fbdev may not be suitable or necessary. So inititally try starting to desktop without stopping at root console. **
-----

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-07-31
> **dsbrooks said:**
> Thanks. I should have replied earlier, I was able to get it to work. The issue I had is that my LiveCD doesn't have a xorg.conf file so I had to generate one via "Xorg -configure :1" and then modify the device info as above. With that I was able to get it running.

I assume fbdev is non-accelerated graphics, so eventually want to try to get the right NVIDIA drivers installed.

I don't have networking for some reason so am working on that. And also need to clean everything up so it boots reliably each time.

There is often no xorg.conf or an empty one, the hardware detects the driver.
An xorg.conf created by nano as above i.e. with just the Section "Device", is sufficient, there is no need to generate one first.

You should always try without editing, using the default video driver, which should be detected for your hardware. The fbdev  driver  may be t wrong for some, for which it is wrong to stop at root console using the 'single' option if the console text is hidden - you get stuck there. This happens for Ubuntu with Radeon 2600HD on imac, where the radeon driver works with efi, but fbdev hangs X.

Possibly the Xserve3,1 with Gop and other refinements will support the Nvidia driver with efi boot.

### Post by pxwpxw on 2009-08-01
Booting ubuntu live iso with grub.efi

Should also work for grub2-pc, with pc options replacing the efi options.
Useful for Xserve, MacBookAir, external drives and to avoid burning CD.

uses ubuntu iso-scan/filename=/u904-64-live.iso

grub2.efi, on apple imac81,

booted u904-amd64-live.iso with grub.efi on imac86
using grub loopback and the ubuntu option 'iso-scan/filename=....iso'

iso was at /dev/sda4/u904-64-live.iso

```

menuentry "u904-64-live.iso" {
 fakebios
 search --set -f /u904-64-live.iso
 loopback loop /u904-64-live.iso
 linux (loop)/casper/vmlinuz boot=casper iso-scan/filename=/u904-64-live.iso video=efifb noefi
 initrd (loop)/casper/initrd.gz
}

```

(fakebios,video=efifb, noefi are grub.efi options).

Note that iso was not found when on hfsplus (although installers do find iso's on hfplsu), had to move to ext3 , probably ok on fat32.

Very good reference found -
[http://www.panticz.de/MultiBootUSB](http://www.panticz.de/MultiBootUSB)
MultiBoot USB with Grub2 (boot directly from iso files)

### Post by dsbrooks on 2009-08-03

> There is often no xorg.conf or an empty one, the hardware detects the driver.
An xorg.conf created by nano as above i.e. with just the Section "Device", is sufficient, there is no need to generate one first.

You should always try without editing, using the default video driver, which should be detected for your hardware. The fbdev  driver  may be t wrong for some, for which it is wrong to stop at root console using the 'single' option if the console text is hidden - you get stuck there. This happens for Ubuntu with Radeon 2600HD on imac, where the radeon driver works with efi, but fbdev hangs X.

Possibly the Xserve3,1 with Gop and other refinements will support the Nvidia driver with efi boot.

I tried without an xorg.conf file and as I recall got garbage on the screen. I tried just a minimal driver block with fbdev and got X11 running but no mouse/keyboard. 

Xorg -config :1 plus fbdev edits worked great.

From there I was able to load the NVIDIA drivers and got accelerated video and 3D.

The last piece for functionality was downloading the latest Intel e1000/e1000e drivers and building and installing it to get networking.

So bean's grub.efi + correct menuentry for boot params + NVIDIA drivers + Intel e1000e driver seems to be the magic recipe for Xserve3,1.
